posts.caption:
The legendary British rock band @therollingstones is reportedly set to release their 25th studio album, Foreign Tongues, on 10 July. Along with the comeback announcement, the biggest attention has been drawn to the new album cover art. The cover was created by American artist Nathaniel Mary Quinn, who is well known for his Cubism-inspired, surreal portrait works.
Due to its distinctive visual style, it may at first glance appear to be AI-generated, but it is in fact entirely hand-made. The artist used charcoal, gouache, pastels, and oil paint to personally depict the faces of the Rolling Stones members through painting and drawing.
The Rolling Stones‚Äô signature rebellious and raw image, combined with Nathaniel Mary Quinn‚Äôs unpolished yet meticulously constructed artwork, is being praised as a work that best captures the band‚Äôs long-lasting vitality and energy.
Words by Julie Jeong
Next-Level Fashion. Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.
ūüď∑ @therollingstones, @artspubliclife
@nathanielmaryquinn #TheRollingStones #NathanielMaryQuinn

posts.caption:
Do you remember the Inflatable Boots presented at Rick Owens‚Äô AW24? These boots are known to have been created in collaboration with London-based designer @straytukay. If you look through his IG, it becomes naturally clear why he was chosen by @rickowensonline. Rather than simply producing visually provocative forms, he uses latex as a material to dramatically reveal the silhouette and tension of the human body. The way he treats the body almost like a sculpture aligns seamlessly with the dark yet futuristic aesthetic that Rick Owens has long explored.
In particular, Straytukay‚Äôs work goes beyond fashion objects, offering an impression that he is investigating the relationship between body and material. Inflated, air-like forms, the skin-tight texture of latex, and exaggerated silhouettes all deliver a sense of unfamiliar tension to the viewer. Perhaps what Rick Owens is looking for is not simply someone who ‚Äúmakes clothes well,‚ÄĚ but a designer capable of fully committing to and pushing forward their own universe. Is this level of intense aesthetic sensibility what it takes to catch Rick Owens‚Äôs eye? Swipe right to explore Straytukay‚Äôs highly refined visuals and distinctive creative world.
Next-Level Fashion. Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.
ūüď∑ @straytukay, @rickowensonline
#Straytukay #RickOwens #RickOwensAW24 #InflatableBoots #LondonDesigner

posts.caption:
As teased yesterday, the @Adidas FIFA World Cup 2026 campaign film has now been released. A 5-minute film goes beyond a traditional brand campaign, focusing instead on the most essential emotion in football ‚ÄĒ pure joy and freedom. Rather than pressure or results, it highlights the inspiration that comes from the game itself, delivering a message that pushes players to go further.
Featuring @tchalamet, @leomessi, @badbunnypr , @lamineyamal , @judebellingham , and @trinity_rodman , the film brings together figures who each define their era in their respective fields. They weave football‚Äôs influence‚ÄĒexpanded into music, film, fashion, and culture at large‚ÄĒinto a single narrative. Ultimately, the film is not about competition or winning. It is a record of emotion, connection, and the inspiration that football continues to create and expand.
Words by Julie Jeong
Next-Level Fashion. Daily.
Follow @a2z_fashionmag Now.
ūüď∑ @adidas
#Adidas #FIFAWorldCup #Timoth√©eChalamet

‚Ā†
Where clothes do appear they rarely take centre stage. Often they are in the process of being removed, revealing the body beneath, or they are part of a witty visual joke. ‚ÄúDoes it have to have clothes in it? Some, maybe ‚Ä¶ maybe a bikini?‚ÄĚ Pfeiffer tells @indiabirgitta.‚Ā†

posts.caption:
Few artists have probed the authorship, seduction and afterlife of circulated images as persistently as American conceptual artist Richard Prince. ‚Ā†
‚Ā†
At the @albertinamuseum in Vienna, a major exhibition brings together some 150 works, many never shown before. Focused on Prince‚Äôs photographic oeuvre from the 1970s to the present, it includes seminal series such as Fashion, Gangs and Cowboys, alongside sculpture, painting, collage and autobiographical imagery. Together, they broaden the view of Prince as a master of appropriation art in photography, dissecting the myths and media codes of American consumer culture.‚Ā†
‚Ā†
‚ÄúPrince analysed how photography transforms our perception of the world,‚ÄĚ Dr Walter Moser, chief curator of photography at Albertina Museum tells @sayori_radda. Photographs do not simply reflect reality, but ‚Äúshape identity, desire and the way we see the world.‚ÄĚ In a culture of copied and circulated images, shaped by misinformation and visual manipulation, Prince‚Äôs question cuts sharply: ‚ÄúIf the copy is the original, what does that make the original?‚ÄĚ‚Ā†
‚Ā†
Read more at the link in bio ūüď≤‚Ā†
‚Ā†
ūüďł Courtesy of Richard Prince Studio ¬© #RichardPrince‚Ā†

posts.caption:
At the heart of @petrafcollins‚Äô practice is a fascination with the fantasies and fears that shape young womanhood, both online and off.‚Ā†
‚Ā†
Her seventh book ‚Äď and third with @rizzolibooks ‚Äď Star follows a fictional pop girl group, tracing the point at which devotion slips into something darker. With chapters titled after songs by Dido, Britney Spears and The Cardigans, its saccharine early pages are charged with the energy of the 2000s without being expressly placed there. ‚Ā†
‚Ā†
Her most ambitious narrative project to date, Collins has described the book as a rehearsal for her first feature film, complete with an original score by composer Jake Nadrich to accompany its reading. This is what practice looks like when you‚Äôre Petra Collins.‚Ā†
‚Ā†
At the link in bio, she speaks to @orlabeee about fame, fandom and why she‚Äôs now turning to film ūüď≤‚Ā†
‚Ā†

posts.caption:
Wearing @isseymiyakeofficial, @solangeknowles ‚Äď our annniversary issue cover star ‚Äď arrived last night at a black-tie gala marking one hundred years of the @schomburgcenter for Research in Black Culture, where she was awarded a Schomburg Medal of Excellence. ‚Ā†
‚Ā†
A cornerstone of @nypl, the centre is dedicated to collecting, preserving and making accessible materials that document the global Black experience, from rare archives and manuscripts to exhibitions, research and public programmes. It‚Äôs a mission that mirrors Knowles‚Äô own work: through @saintheron, her multidisciplinary platform and archive, she has spent the past decade building spaces for Black cultural memory. ‚Ā†
‚Ā†
‚ÄúA lot of the questions I‚Äôve been asking myself lately surround the urgency of Black women‚Äôs history. It almost feels like survival,‚ÄĚ Solange told AnOther Magazine in our latest issue. Revisit the interview at the link in bio ūüď≤‚Ā†
‚Ā†
ūüďł by @andymartinezx1

posts.caption:
Sudha Reddy (@sudhareddy.official) paid a majestic ode to her home state at the 2026 Met Gala, wearing custom Manish Malhotra (@manishmalhotra05) ‚ÄėTree of Life‚Äô couture that reimagined centuries-old textile tradition belonging to Andhra Pradesh and Telangana. Conceived in collaboration with ace Hollywood stylist Mariel Haenn, the ensemble comprising zardozi, marodi, resham and metalwork celebrated the storied Machilipatnam Kalamkari textile tradition. Realised over 3,459 hours by 90 artisans, the look featured a hand-curated metal upper-back installation in brass, copper and silver embracing motifs including the Palapitta, Tangedu, Jammi Chettu , Surya-Chandra and Kalpavriksha. This triumph of Indian maximalism was anchored by the breathtaking 550-carat ‚ÄėQueen of Merelani‚Äô tanzanite, the centerpiece of the MEIL director‚Äôs estimated $15 million jewellery suite.
#metgala #manishmalhotra #collab #sudhareddy #metgala2026
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by Harper's Bazaar India on May 07, 2026 tagging @sudhareddy.official. May be an image of shawl, magazine, dress and text.

posts.caption:
Fast fashion, once firmly positioned outside the realm of high fashion credibility, is finding increasingly strategic ways to enter the Met Gala conversation. Not through mass market replication, but through reinvention, collaboration, and carefully calibrated moments that blur the boundaries between accessibility and aspiration. From Kendall Jenner (@kendalljenner) in GapStudio (@gap) sculptural reimagining of the white T shirt, to Bad Bunny (@badbunnypr) in Zara (@zara), and Jimmy Butler (@jimmybutler) bringing Alo Yoga (@aloyoga) onto fashion‚Äôs most prestigious platform, these brands are carving out space within a landscape that once seemed entirely out of reach.
Tap the link in bio to read the full story.

posts.caption:
Change doesn‚Äôt always begin in cities.
Sometimes, it starts at the roots.
Through her work with rural communities, Rajshri Deshpande (@rajshri_deshpande) is helping reimagine what villages can become‚ÄĒself-reliant, sustainable, and thriving. From water security to education and livelihoods, the focus is on building ecosystems that empower communities from within.
Because when villages grow stronger, everything else follows.
#BeRememberedForGood #100PipersBeRememberedForGood #BeRememberedForGoodStories

posts.caption:
For decades, India‚Äôs craft traditions have quietly shaped the language of global luxury, often referenced, occasionally replicated, but rarely centred. At the Met Gala 2026, that dynamic felt notably different, as Indian craft emerged not merely as embellishment, but as art in its own right. From Diya Mehta Jatia (@dmjatia) in Mayyur Girotra (@mayyurgirotra)‚Äôs revival of Bengal‚Äôs Shola craft and Karan Johar‚Äôs painterly Manish Malhotra (@manishmalhotra05)‚Äôs moment to Sudha Reddy (@sudhareddy.official)‚Äôs Kalamkari-inspired couture and Rihanna (@badgalriri)‚Äôs Gem Palace jewels, the red carpet became a powerful expression of India‚Äôs heritage, artistry, and evolving global design language.
Tap the link in bio to read the full story.

posts.caption:
Over her stellar 20-year career, @phoebejtonkin has captivated audiences. The Sydney-born, New York-based actor began acting as a teenager playing a mermaid in the series ‚ÄėH2O: Just Add Water,‚Äô and has since achieved a dynamic array of dramatic parts, a stint in the director‚Äôs chair and a new role producing and starring in the adaptation of crime thriller, ‚ÄėThe Dark Lake‚Äô. While she‚Äôs relishing the collaborative process, the actor and May 2026 cover star tells BAZAAR, acting remains her first love.‚ÄúI feel really lucky to be invited into the collaborative process so early on in a project. Usually, as an actor, you turn up and you‚Äôre told, ‚ÄėThis is what your set looks like, these are your fellow actors‚Äô. And with this, I get to be there from the inception of the project and be part of these decisions.‚ÄĚ
Through the link in bio, Phoebe Tonkin reflects on her career and attraction to robust roles that ‚Äúlean into the complexities of being a woman.‚ÄĚ

‚ÄúI‚Äôm attracted to things that are authentic and grounded,‚ÄĚ @phoebejtonkin tells BAZAAR of her latest role in the cross-genre ‚ÄėTwo Years Later‚Äô, a post-pandemic romantic dramedy set in Brisbane. Tonkin plays Emily, a woman with a complicated love and family life who sets out on a series of dates with Ryan, played by Brenton Thwaites, to work out if they‚Äôre destined to marry. ‚ÄúThere was something quite relatable and ordinary about these two people who are in their 30s and trying to navigate dating, coming out of a pandemic, all the isolation and loneliness that they‚Äôve been dealing with. And I just thought it was really real,‚ÄĚ she shares. It was this funny, quirky love story drama that you don‚Äôt get to do often.‚ÄĚ

posts.caption:
Since the earliest days of tools like ChatGPT and Claude, industry conversations have been marked by a tension between excitement around speed and efficiency alongside deep-seated fears of job loss, creative dilution and concerns about its environmental footprint. What once played out in theory is now unfolding in practice ‚ÄĒ as a broader rejection of what AI represents ‚ÄĒ particularly as more consumers view AI-generated content as a cost-cutting measure that erodes fashion‚Äôs human touch,
In this episode, The Debrief host Sheena Butler-Young (@sheena.butler.young) discusses with BoF correspondents Marc Bain (@marcbain_) and Haley Crawford (@haleycrawf) why the backlash is intensifying and how consumer sentiment against brands using AI-generated imagery is forcing a reckoning. They explore whether fashion can actually embrace these tools without losing the care and time that confers luxury status.
ūüéôÔłŹ Listen now #linkinbio #TheDebriefPodcast
ūüď∑ Getty Images

posts.caption:
Writer, academic and activist Sin√©ad Burke (@thesineadburke) has donated two pieces from her private wardrobe to the permanent collection of the Met Museum‚Äôs Costume Institute, including a custom-made Burberry trench coat, scissored and shot by Tim Walker (@timwalker) for the cover of BoF‚Äôs ‚ÄėThe Age of Influence‚Äô special print issue in 2018.
The trench appears in the department‚Äôs latest exhibition, ‚ÄėCostume Art,‚Äô which was unveiled with last night‚Äôs Met Gala and is, in part, organised around a series of thematic body types, including ‚Äėthe disabled body.‚Äô Burke‚Äôs own body was scanned to develop the mannequin on which the coat is displayed.
‚ÄúThis is an explicit and radical invitation for disabled people to see themselves in fashion, to see themselves in the Met, to see themselves at the Costume Institute,‚ÄĚ said Burke, who consulted on the exhibition.
Watch how the first mannequin of Burke‚Äôs body was created on The BoF Show‚Äôs third episode ‚ÄúEverybody is Different. Why Does Fashion Always Look the Same?‚ÄĚ #linkinbio

posts.caption:
Anna Wintour‚Äôs nearly 30 years leading the Met Gala ‚ÄĒ the annual fundraiser for the Metropolitan Museum of Art‚Äôs Costume Institute ‚ÄĒ has been an effort to prove fashion‚Äôs legitimacy as an art form.
This year, the Costume Institute declared that milestone accomplished, opening its new permanent home inside the museum, the Cond√© M. Nast Galleries, a 10,000 square-foot space located just off the Great Hall. Beyond the new galleries, this year‚Äôs Met Gala raised a record $42 million.
Just a few weeks ago, though, the Met Gala‚Äôs success didn‚Äôt seem certain.
BoF‚Äôs Joan Kennedy (@joooankennedyyy) explores how the event overcame the backlash against sponsors Jeff and Lauren S√°nchez Bezos to pull off a record-breaking night. Read more #linkinbio
ūüď∑ Getty Images, @joooankennedyyy

posts.caption:
For four decades, Dries Van Noten defined a singular path in global fashion with a universe rooted in intellectual rigour, exquisite craftsmanship and independence. When he stepped back from his eponymous brand last year, it wasn‚Äôt a retreat into a quiet retirement. Instead, Van Noten has embarked on a profound transition ‚ÄĒ moving from the relentless, dictated rhythm of fashion to a new life as a custodian of culture in Venice.
Van Noten has established a new foundation (@fondazionedriesvannoten) at the Palazzo Pisani Moretta, a space dedicated to the beauty of craftsmanship and the belief that in a world marked by global uncertainty, the act of making something beautiful is the ultimate form of protest.
In this special episode of The BoF Podcast, our editor-at-large Tim Blanks (@timblanks) speaks to Dries Van Noten about this remarkable transition to becoming a custodian of beauty.
ūüéôÔłŹ Listen now #linkinbio #TheBoFPodcast
ūüď∑ Fe Pinheiro

posts.caption:
As the initial results of the creative change sweeping the fashion industry have begun to trickle in, Matthieu Blazy‚Äôs Chanel revamp cemented its position at the front of the pack this week, topping the revamped Lyst Index, a measure of brand heat.
Since its October reveal, the style reboot at the French fashion giant has stood out amid the sector-wide reset, dominating discussions online and sparking shopping frenzies in boutiques.
BoF's Mimosa Spencer explores whether the popularity of Blazy‚Äôs revamp raises questions about brand heat ‚ÄĒ and the risk of a rapid cool-down if ‚ÄúBlazymania‚ÄĚ goes unmanaged. Read more #linkinbio
ūüď∑ @chanelofficial

posts.caption:
Generally speaking, you can't sit on the art in a museum. But in one gallery of the Museum of Contemporary Art Chicago (@mcachicago) ‚ÄĒ which is currently staged to resemble a karaoke bar complete with a disco ball, stage and jukebox ‚ÄĒ three plastic chairs, upholstered with the face of Puerto Rican superstar @badbunnypr, are waiting for you to rest between songs.
@edrasoto's "BB Chairs" ‚ÄĒ outfitted in bootleg fabrics featuring the singer with sunglasses and buzz cuts ‚ÄĒ have been a tongue-in-cheek nod to both the plastic white chair ubiquitous to the island and the performer's deep connection to his home.
"I had this idea a whole year before I made them," she said. "I was doubting myself. I was thinking maybe this is too on the nose."
But friends excitedly reached out to Soto when Bad Bunny released the now history-making, @grammys Award-winning album "DeB√ć TiRAR M√°S FOToS." The album cover featured two empty white lawn chairs ‚ÄĒ an evocative symbol of home and belonging in Puerto Rico ‚ÄĒ and they had significance to Soto's practice, too.
"The furniture that I grew up with was wicker and plastic," she explained. "I asked myself what my chair would look like if I was making a chair." She said she couldn't relate to high-end materials, and began thinking about the fantasy of luxury in both the practice of upholstery and the colorful, if culturally inaccurate, images associated with the tropics.
ūüďł : Courtesy the artist/E G Schempf/Courtesy Kemper Museum of Contemporary Art
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by CNN Style on May 07, 2026 tagging @mcachicago, and @edrasoto. May be pop art of armchair, rocking chair, poster, chair and text that says 'THE BAD BUNNY CHAIRS TAKING OVER THE ART WORLD CNN'.

posts.caption:
The return of @bts.bighitofficial in March, nearly four years after they went on hiatus for mandatory military service, has highlighted a key question for the K-pop industry: What comes next?
This current generation has arguably grander ambitions ‚Äď some of which beg the question: Does K-pop need to be Korean at all?
For instance, @blackpinkofficial's recently released mini-album "Deadline" is almost entirely in English. And new groups are debuting in other parts of the world, some with members that have no ties to South Korea.
"There's more global collaboration, and work within the K-pop world to become globalized, within every generation," said Danny Chung, the songwriter behind several major Blackpink hits.
Some of this is happening in other musical genres, too, with social media increasingly shaping how we consume and produce music. But it's especially true of K-pop, which is "ever-changing," said Chung, who also works in the Artists and Repertoire (A&R) team for record company The Black Label.
Experts say this fluidity could fuel the industry's aim to expand beyond borders and neat categorization.
"It's a forever moving target to try to define the genre of K-pop," Chung said.
ūüďł : Kim Hong-Ji/Pool/Getty Images/Christopher Polk/Billboard/Andrew Caballero-Reynolds/AFP

posts.caption: Couture math is insane. Margot Robbie's 2026 Met Gala Chanel gown required 761 hours of handwork and contains 1,080 individually embroidered elements. posts.caption:
‚ÄúNobody does it like her.‚ÄĚ
Nobody makes dark femininity feel as instinctive as Mariacarla Boscono. The sharp silhouettes, the restrained glamour, the balance between softness and severity ‚ÄĒ it all feels natural on her. In Of Love and Other Demons for d la Repubblica, photographed by Carlijn Jacobs, Mariacarla moves through sculptural looks and cinematic beauty with the kind of presence that can‚Äôt be taught.
That‚Äôs what has made her so influential in fashion for decades. Since the late 1990s, Mariacarla has become one of the industry‚Äôs most recognizable and respected faces, working with designers including Riccardo Tisci, Karl Lagerfeld, Prada, Givenchy, Dior, Valentino, McQueen, and Chanel. More than just a model, she represents a certain kind of fashion image: intelligent, emotional, slightly undone, and never overly polished.
Long before dark feminine aesthetics became mainstream again, Mariacarla was already defining them. Her ability to bring character and emotion into an editorial is what continues to make her relevant across generations of fashion.
Because nobody does it like her.
‚ÄĒ
Editorial Credits
Model: Mariacarla Boscono
Photography: Carlijn Jacobs
Styling: Jacob K
Hair: Oliver Schwalder
Makeup: Lucy Bridge
Publication: d la Repubblica
Issue: October 19, 2024
Editorial Title: Of Love and Other Demons

posts.caption:
The Misshapes sit in a very specific corner of mid-2000s New York nightlife: post-electroclash, pre-Instagram, when a party could still feel like a private scene even while being photographed every week. With Geordon Nicol, Leigh Lezark, and Greg Krelenstein at the center, The Misshapes became less about one venue or one night and more about a certain downtown visual code.
The look was narrow, dark, and intentionally unfinished. Think black skinny jeans, shrunken leather jackets, smudged eyeliner, slept-in hair, etc. ‚ÄĒ but not in a polished ‚Äústyled for a theme party‚ÄĚ way. It was not Y2K in the glossy pop-star sense. It was colder, cheaper-looking, more nocturnal; like someone had crossed a fashion closet with a basement bar.
The dress code worked because it never looked fully styled. A designer piece could sit next to something thrifted, and the whole outfit had the feeling of being caught between a gallery opening, a cigarette outside, and a 3 a.m. flash photo. The attitude was just as important as the clothes: bored, angular, slightly inaccessible.
That is what makes the Misshapes reference still interesting now. It is not just ‚Äúindie sleaze‚ÄĚ as a trend, but a record of a moment when nightlife, fashion, photography, and social hierarchy all collapsed into one image. The clothes were simple, but the context made them feel coded. You were not just dressing for a party ‚ÄĒ you were dressing for the possibility of being documented.
In that sense, the Misshapes dress code was never really about looking perfect. It was about looking like you belonged to a scene that was already halfway mythologizing itself.
Important photo credits / references
* The Misshapes book photography contributors: Julian Gilbert, Karl Lagerfeld, Leigh Lezark, Nick Haymes, Miguel Villalobos, Patrick McMullan, Ryan McGinley, Ryan Pfluger, Scott Meriam, and Nick Zinner.

posts.caption:
This editorial presents Anja Rubik and Ibrahim Kamara in a poetic, gender-fluid visual dialogue for the May 2020 collaboration between Vogue Italia and L‚ÄôUomo Vogue. Photographed by Harley Weir, the story is built around the idea of mirroring: masculine and feminine codes reflect, overlap, and dissolve into one another. Vogue Italia describes the project as two complementary fashion stories across the women‚Äôs and men‚Äôs magazines, exploring gender in fashion and society through ‚Äúa game of mirrors.‚ÄĚ
The images feel intimate, theatrical, and quietly radical. Rubik and Kamara appear as both individuals and alter egos, moving through sharply styled tailoring, sculptural silhouettes, exposed skin, headpieces, and graphic beauty details. Kamara‚Äôs styling uses fashion as a language of transformation, placing classic menswear, couture-like gestures, and body-conscious styling into a space where identity becomes fluid rather than fixed. The result is less a conventional fashion editorial than a visual conversation about self-recognition, performance, and becoming.
Harley Weir‚Äôs photography gives the story a dreamlike tension: soft yet direct, sensual yet cerebral. The editorial‚Äôs strength lies in its balance between vulnerability and control. Rubik‚Äôs presence is fierce and statuesque, while Kamara brings a personal, performative energy that makes the story feel like an exchange between artist, stylist, model, and subject. Together, they create an editorial that treats gender not as a binary, but as a living, shifting surface‚ÄĒsomething seen, styled, reflected, and reimagined.
Credits: Photography: Harley Weir. Styling/Fashion Editor: Ibrahim ‚ÄúIb‚ÄĚ Kamara. Models: Anja Rubik and Ibrahim Kamara. Make-up: Hiromi Ueda, using Le Volume Stretch de Chanel and Boy de Chanel Le Teint. Hair: Cyndia Harvey, using Oribe. Manicure: Sylvie MacMillan. Set Design: Alice Kirkpatrick. Bondage Artists: Anna Barros and Erlend Davidson at Anatomie Studio. On Set: Shiny Projects. Stylist Assistant: Sasha Harris. Casting Director: Piergiorgio Del Moro. Editors: Emanuele Farneti and Thomas Persson. Publications: Vogue Italia and L‚ÄôUomo Vogue, May 2020.

posts.caption:
Born in Jamaica and raised between strict religion, rebellion, and reinvention, she became one of those rare figures who made Fashion feel bigger than just clothing. In the 1970s, she moved through New York and Paris as a model, but she never fit the soft, pretty mold the industry expected from women at that time. That was the point. Her beauty was sharp, sculptural, and impossible to soften.
Grace turned her body into architecture. The flat-top hair, the power shoulders, the dark sunglasses, the sharp tailoring, the metallic makeup. She played with masculinity and femininity before fashion had comfortable language for it. One moment she looked like a goddess, the next like a nightclub villain, the next like a living sculpture.
What made her different was that she didn‚Äôt wear fashion to look desirable. She wore it to look powerful. Designers, photographers, and artists didn‚Äôt just dress her, they built worlds around her. With Jean-Paul Goude, her image became surreal, graphic, almost superhuman. With designers like Azzedine Ala√Įa, her strength, shape, and confidence became part of the clothes themselves.
Her life moved between music, modeling, film, art, and performance, but fashion was always the thread connecting everything. She made disco feel dangerous, androgyny feel luxurious, and glamour feel untouchable.
Grace Jones didn‚Äôt become a fashion icon because she followed style.
She became one because she made style obey her.
posts.alt_text: Photo by CULT MAGAZINE‚ďá on May 04, 2026. May be an image of one or more people, makeup, hair, people standing, magazine, poster and text that says 'The Woman who defined an Entire Era. CULT'.

posts.caption:
Spring 2026 could be looking icy in the best way.
It‚Äôs that cold, clean mix of black leather, sheer tights, silver details, slick hair and sharp little glasses. Everything feels a bit polished, a bit extra, but still cool.
The colors stay simple: black, white, grey, silver and icy blue. But the textures make it interesting. Glossy leather, soft fur, shiny bags, thin tights, metal details. It‚Äôs not too sweet, not too pretty, not trying too hard.
It feels like office siren‚Äôs colder sister, a little more mysterious, a little more dressed-up.
Less spring flowers, more frozen city girl.
*This Post is meant to serve as inspiration*

posts.caption:
The 80s remain one of fashion‚Äôs most recognizable references, not because of nostalgia alone, but because the decade understood impact. In ‚ÄěCollections‚Äú for Vogue Japan March 2018, Luigi & Iango bring that energy into a contemporary context: sharp shoulders, rich textures, bold styling and a sense of glamour that feels confident.
The editorial plays with classic 80s codes without making them look like costume. Anna Dello Russo‚Äôs styling mixes structure, shine, lace, leather and exaggerated silhouettes in a way that feels controlled and editorial. The result is dramatic, but never messy ‚ÄĒ a reminder that excess can still be precise.
Hair and makeup complete the mood with volume, definition and attitude, while the models carry the clothes with the kind of presence the era demands. It feels powerful, visual and slightly theatrical, but still refined. This is why the 80s continue to influence fashion: they gave designers permission to be bold, and editorials like this show how modern that boldness can still feel.
Editorial: Collections
Magazine: Vogue Japan, March 2018

posts.caption:
For years, the diamond midstream ran on one big assumption:
Prices will always go up.
That assumption is now costing the industry.
In this sharp reality check, Pranay Narvekar, Partner at Pharos Beam Consulting, breaks down why inventory discipline ‚ÄĒ not speculation ‚ÄĒ will define survival and growth.
Here‚Äôs what‚Äôs really happening:
The midstream is overcrowded.
Margins are under pressure.
Demand is shifting structurally.
And holding inventory is no longer ‚Äúsafe.‚ÄĚ
Add to that lab-grown disruption, China slowdown, US uncertainties and the message is clear:
ūüĎČ The rules have changed.
This is no longer a volume game.
This is a strategy, discipline, and efficiency game.
If you‚Äôre still buying based on ‚Äúfuture price increase,‚ÄĚ
you‚Äôre already behind.
Save this. Share this. This is a mindset shift the industry can‚Äôt ignore.
#DiamondIndustry #JewelleryIndustry #B2BJewellery #DiamondTrade

posts.caption:
The fatal outbreak of hantavirus aboard a cruise ship sailing the Atlantic reportedly may have been contracted during a Dutch couple‚Äôs bird-watching trip at a landfill aka people playing in literal trash ūüôÉ
Hantavirus is a rare, deadly disease primarily carried by infected rodents. So far, three passengers on the Oceanwide Expeditions cruise ship have died, with at least four others showing symptoms, according to the World Health Organization (WHO).
A KLM flight attendant who was in contact with a now deceased passenger is also showing symptoms and is being tested for hantavirus, authorities in the Netherlands have confirmed.
On Thursday, Oceanwide issued an update saying 30 guests disembarked the ship on April 24, nearly two weeks after the first passenger died on April 11. According to Oceanwide, the ship was carrying 88 passengers and 61 crew members.
Despite the outbreak, WHO insists there‚Äôs no need to panic, saying ‚Äúthe risk to the global population from this event as low.‚ÄĚ
The US completed its withdrawal from the WHO in Jan 2026, following an Executive Order signed by President Trump on his first day in office last year. Oh, he also gutted the CDC, but no need to worry since RFK is at the helm now ::screams into pillow::
#hantavirus #threads #meme #dump #news

posts.caption:
This year‚Äôs Met Gala didn‚Äôt feel as fun as it used to. That‚Äôs in no small part due to the involvement of the Bezos‚Äôs, for whom the event has been dubbed the ‚ÄúTech Gala.‚ÄĚ But is the Tech Gala just a symptom of a disease that‚Äôs been festering for a long time now?
When the Met Gala began in 1948, it wasn‚Äôt the press-addled, celeb-fueled circus it is today, but was an elite dinner that benefitted the Met‚Äôs Costume Institute. In 1973, Diana Vreeland, Editor-in-Chief of Vogue, became involved and the event received its first splashy theme in honor of Balenciaga who had died the year previous. One year later, Cher wore the nude dress to the Gala which drew an unprecedented heap of press.
Cut to the Anna Wintour era when the event began to include stars from all walks of fame, and then to the 2010s when it became a mainstream pop culture moment. In 2015 the Gala not only came with its own documentary, but Rihanna wore that yellow Guo Pei gown with a 16‚Äô train. The dress caused a media frenzy, transforming the way celebs went ‚Äėall out‚Äô for the event. And, in turn, it transformed the way the public engaged online: Social media commentary became commonplace, if not ubiquitous, forcing the event to inflate into the spectacle it is today.
But, because the Gala became the de facto measure of cultural clout, tech bro culture vultures bought their way onto the guest list this year. Their presence, though, was so unpopular that they couldn‚Äôt even show face on the carpet and an inordinate amount of protests cropped up. The public perception of the event took a loathsome turn, with many online comparing the event to The Hunger Games. Even some of the Gala‚Äôs favorite celebs dropped out for suspicious reasons.
Is there a future for this once glorious night?
Speaking about the Gala, Andrew Bolton, head of the Costume Institute, admitted ‚Äúsometimes it dwarfs everything.‚ÄĚ Thus, the Institute has saved up their proceeds and are working towards self-sufficiency. That means that the event may not be the philanthropic gesture that it once was, making it instead just a blown up photo-op.
But, where does that leave us? The spectators en masse? Have we grown out of it too?
posts.alt_text: Photo by Diet Prada ‚ĄĘ on May 06, 2026. May be an image of costume, poster, magazine, headdress, crowd and text that says 'The Rise and Fall ofthe Met Gala The event has outgrown itself, the Costume Institute seems to have outgrown the event, and the public is no longer buying it. COSTUME ART Diet PRADA'.

posts.caption:
Amidst a sea of lavish gowns at the Met Gala, nearly all of which were custom pieces crafted by the most prolific fashion houses on the planet, one look stood out for the exact opposite reason; Bhavitha Mandava appeared to be wearing a casual quarter-zip with straight cut blue jeans.
To be fair, there‚Äôs more to the look than meets the eye. First of all, the outfit is Chanel and it is, technically, an illusion made from silk, not denim. Second, it calls back to the outfit Mandava, a grad student at NYU, was wearing on the day she entered the Atlantic Avenue subway station and was ‚Äėdiscovered‚Äô by a modeling agent. She became a bright star in the modeling world at a meteoric pace, and in March of this year became the first Indian woman to hold a coveted Chanel ambassadorship.
But, the Met Gala look isn‚Äôt the first time Chanel has reinterpreted Mandava‚Äôs fated subway station look; she opened Chanel‚Äôs Pre-Fall ‚Äė26 show, which was held on a defunct NYC subway platform, in a recreation of the same outfit. Why, then, was it necessary to churn the same look out at such an important event as the Met Gala?
Some fans, especially those with Indian and South Asian heritage, are enraged, calling the outfit ‚Äúracist‚ÄĚ and ‚Äúa microaggression‚ÄĚ and pointing out that the outfit would not have been given to a white model or ambassador. IG fashion commentator Sufi Motiwala said he was ‚Äúpissed‚ÄĚ and accused Chanel of ‚Äútokenizing‚ÄĚ Mandava.
‚ÄúBe serious,‚ÄĚ said Anisha Ramakrishna Tarpara on IG, ‚Äúthe outfit to get on the subway, the outfit I wear to go to CVS. Chanel really said ‚Äėwe see India and we want to meet you underground.‚Äô‚ÄĚ
The overly casual look which the design house has prioritized for their first ever Indian ambassador seems to peddle a narrative which excludes her from the glamour of her counterparts. Mandava‚Äôs Chanel counterparts, by the way, include Jennie, Margot Robbie, Lily Rose-Depp, Gracie Abrams, and Anna Wintour herself who each wore luxurious evening gowns to the Gala. As did Awar Odhiang, a fellow Chanel model of comparable viral fame. In a bathroom selfie taken by Mandava, the discrepancy of her jeans-and-a-casual-top outfit is impossible to ignore.
Thoughts?
posts.alt_text: Photo by Diet Prada ‚ĄĘ on May 05, 2026. May be an image of magazine and text that says 'Was Chanel's Met Gala Look for Bhavitha Mandava Racist? Chanel dressed their first-ever Indian ambassador in an extremely casual look for fashion's biggest night. Diet PRADA'.

posts.caption:
Welp, fashion‚Äôs biggest night came and went, but the backlash is still making the rounds.
Critiquing the ultra-wealthy comes up basically every year, but this gala hit different with the Bezos‚Äô underwriting the whole spectacle.
There‚Äôs been blowback online since Jeff and Lauren Bezos‚Äô sponsorship was announced in November, the couple reportedly spent $10 million to sponsor the event. Yesterday added marches in the streets, footage calling out the billionaire projected on his $120M Manhattan penthouse, hidden bottles of fake urine around the Met, and a counter-fashion show with Amazon workers as the models.
Swipe through to see what else happened on the first Monday in May.

posts.caption:
It‚Äôs the first Monday in May! What will you be up to this evening? Chances are, you won‚Äôt be at the Met‚Ä¶ at least not physically. Chances are you‚Äôll be there in spirit and on Instagram, as is the tradition on this storied holiday for fashion folks and pop culture followers.
But this year feels a little different because many are calling for a boycott of the Gala. Jeff Bezos and his wife Lauren Sanchez are sponsors and co-chairs this year (not exactly for the first time, as Jeff was an honorary chair in 2012) which feels like it crosses a line for audiences who have, in years past, interacted with the festivities despite a myriad of controversies. Who can forget the anxiety surrounding 2015‚Äôs ‚ÄúChina: Through The Looking Glass‚ÄĚ theme? Or the debates about Karl Lagerfeld‚Äôs history of troubling remarks when he was honored in 2023? This year, because of Bezos, protests are being planned and ad hoc demonstrations have already been cropping up all over NYC, courtesy of a group called ‚ÄúEveryone Hates Elon‚ÄĚ.
How does that affect you? The audiences at home? Are you still planning to keep up with the outfits? Will you post about your favorite (and maybe also your least favorite) stars? Afterall, the Gala exists to benefit the Met‚Äôs Costume Institute. Many online are pointing out that supporting the arts is net positive, regardless of where the money is coming from. The costume institute is, afterall, the only wing of the Met that must fund itself.
Others, though, are exhausted by the state of the economy and the price of gas and groceries. And, in light of the wars our country is participating in overseas, not everyone is able to justify participating in such a flashy show of wealth and celebrity. That‚Äôs saying nothing of their distrust (and disgust) for tech industry billionaires and their growing resentment towards tech‚Äôs involvement in fashion.
So, is ‚Äėboycotting‚Äô the #MetGala a slap in the face of all the work the institute does to preserve garments with profound historical significance? Or is it an honest refusal of tech oligarchy that can help shape Met Galas in years to come?
The jury is out. How will you be engaging (or not) with the Gala this evening?

posts.caption:
I don‚Äôt think it‚Äôs racism, and she wouldn‚Äôt be a Chanel girl in the first place if there were any racism in play, and this isn‚Äôt me trying to defend anybody because Chanel doesn‚Äôt need defending. What I feel is that there was an ‚Äúidea‚ÄĚ there, a thought that maybe didn‚Äôt translate, it probably felt stronger as an idea to give a couture version, the highest form of handcraft, of her ‚Äúgenesis look‚ÄĚ, but it‚Äôs coming across as basic to mangoes and everyone watching. So, what‚Äôs happened is that the idea existed but just didn‚Äôt translate onto the carpet at all, especially when everyone else is wearing mega gowns and showing off, this, to the world-at-large, is not going to land. She looks like ‚Ä¶ like she‚Äôs going to the mall in haute couture.
I‚Äôm the number one hater of things, but I feel like we need to give her some grace, because in hating Chanel we‚Äôre actually piling onto her. No more notes

Every time you can‚Äôt be right and here, you‚Äôre clearly mistaken. Pacho isn‚Äôt a boy anymore, and yes, the beard is intentional, in many circles it‚Äôs considered stylish, while a clean-shaven look can feel a bit dated.
As for the ‚Äúpoochistani‚ÄĚ saree comment, I think there‚Äôs some misunderstanding. It would help to read a bit more about sarees and their evolution in fashion. She isn‚Äôt dressing according to an ‚Äúancestor‚ÄĚ theme.. gawar she‚Äôs actually aligning with a flowing, artistic fashion concept. The look draws inspiration from how Maharani Gayatri Devi carried herself, blending tradition with elegance.

posts.caption:
At the intersection of traditional craftsmanship and modern sculpture sat business tycoon Sudha Reddy‚Äôs 2026 Met Gala look‚ÄĒa living cartography of her South Indian lineage. Titled ‚ÄėThe Tree of Life,‚Äô this custom Manish Malhotra couture, styled by Hollywood‚Äôs favourite Mariel Haenn, served as a living map of Kalamkari artistry, specifically the Machilipatnam tradition of hand-painted storytelling. Every inch of the ensemble‚ÄĒmeticulously rendered in zardozi, marodi, resham and mixed metal work over 3,459 hours by 90 artisans was embedded with symbolic national motifs like the Palapitta, Tangedu and Jammi Chettu, alongside celestial icons like the Kalpavriksha and Surya-Chandra. Her estimated $15 million jewelry suite was anchored by the breathtaking ‚ÄėQueen of Merelani,‚Äô a 550-carat deep violet-blue tanzanite sourced from Tanzania‚Äôs Merelani Hills.
#metgala  #sudhareddy #manishmalhotra  #metgala2026

posts.caption:
Lights, camera, @billieeilish.
At just 24 years old, the 10-time Grammy winner and two-time Oscar winner has existed in the public consciousness for a decade now. But this May, a new concert documentary attempts to capture her star power and stage presence in a new light‚ÄĒthrough the eyes of legendary director James Cameron, no less. Eilish never thought she‚Äôd make another documentary after 2021‚Äôs ‚ÄėBillie Eilish: The World‚Äôs a Little Blurry,‚Äô which closely documented her adolescence and the making of her sensational 2019 debut album. ‚ÄúI‚Äôm so private about my actual life now,‚ÄĚ Eilish tells ELLE, but when the ‚ÄėAvatar‚Äô filmmaker came calling, she knew she had to pick up‚ÄĒand co-direct the film alongside him.
In ELLE‚Äôs May 2026 cover story, Eilish opens up about collaborating with Cameron, how her personal and professional relationship with her brother @finneas has evolved, and her activist spirit: ‚ÄúI make art and music to reach people, to help people.‚ÄĚ Read the full interview at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
If hopecore is what you're after, look no further. @isabelklee‚ÄĒand her dog, Simon‚ÄĒare here to save your algorithm.
To her audience of over 2 million followers on TikTok and Instagram, Klee offers daily updates as she fosters a series of hard-case medical and behavioral rescue dogs, then eventually sends them along to elated new owners.
Now, Klee‚Äôs first book‚ÄĒa sweet memoir of her twenties in New York called ‚ÄėDogs, Boys, and Other Things I‚Äôve Cried About‚Äô‚ÄĒis a number one New York Times bestseller. In nonlinear chapters alternating between the dogs and the men she‚Äôs loved, Klee tells of dating an addict, then a man who just didn‚Äôt fit, only to self-sabotage when she met her match, now-fianc√© Jacob. During this time, she also met the great love of her life, Simon, a rescue from Korea whose canine idiopathic epilepsy motivated her to help other medically complex fosters.
In conversation with ELLE, Klee unpacks how dogs help make the algorithm a little brighter, what they‚Äôve taught her about love and resilience, and the upcoming TV adaptation of her debut memoir.

posts.caption:
In a time when the world was besotted with European fashion, @ralphlauren helped put American style on the map.
As the brand pored over more than a half-century‚Äôs worth of runway imagery‚ÄĒsome of which hadn‚Äôt been seen by the design team in decades‚ÄĒin preparation for ‚ÄėRalph Lauren Catwalk,‚Äô Lauren‚Äôs staying power was evident. ‚ÄúTimeless‚ÄĚ is a moving target, but the tweed jackets and fringed Western skirts Lauren designed in his early years could easily be on a runway today. ‚ÄúHe was telling stories that were romantic and universal,‚ÄĚ says @davidlauren, the designer‚Äôs son and chief branding and innovation officer. ‚ÄúThe American spirit of the cowboy is not about a guy on a horse. It is about freedom.‚ÄĚ
Take an inside look at the new book‚ÄĒand explore Lauren‚Äôs legacy‚ÄĒat the link in bio.

posts.caption:
96-year-old Dolores Huerta is a mother of 13, a feminist, a civil rights activist‚ÄĒand a survivor.
Earlier this year, when reporters at The New York Times called Huerta for comment on an investigation about allegations of Cesar Chavez‚Äôs abuse of girls and women, she revealed a secret she had held for nearly 60 years‚ÄĒthat he had raped her. The article has spurred a recentering of women in social movements who worked diligently alongside men like Chavez but let them take the credit. But now, with this reckoning, deserving women like Huerta are getting their flowers.
‚ÄúThe achievements won for farm workers and others are still there and cannot be erased,‚ÄĚ Huerta tells ELLE. ‚ÄúThe support and love that I have received in this difficult moment has given me the strength and inspiration to continue working and seeking answers to end violence against women, girls, and boys.‚ÄĚ
In a powerful and moving conversation, Huerta speaks to ELLE about taking on the powerful in the quest for justice, our current political moment, and mobilizing young people to continue her life‚Äôs work and taking charge of their future.
Read the full interview at the link in bio.

A global force shaping the now, Mega Power Star Ram Charan (@alwaysramcharan) is one of the defining faces of Indian cinema today. But beyond the scale and the noise, there‚Äôs a quieter story at play: one built on discipline, instinct and an unrelenting sense of self.
Years into a formidable career, Charan isn‚Äôt chasing expectation‚Ä¶ he‚Äôs refining it. Choosing presence over performance and meaning over mere spectacle.
We celebrate his journey with our latest cover, where the actor steps into a version of himself like never seen before.

posts.caption:
By her third appearance at the Met Gala 2026, Sudha Reddy clearly understood that the best red carpet looks say something before you do. In a custom creation by Manish Malhotra, she brought South Indian craft, memory and symbolism onto fashion‚Äôs biggest stage. Over 90 artisans spent 3,459 hours reinterpreting Machilipatnam Kalamkari through zardozi, marodi, and resham work, while motifs like the Palapitta, Tangedu, Jammi Chettu, and Kalpavriksha carried the story deeper than surface glamour. Then came the final flex: the 550-carat Queen of Merelani tanzanite necklace. The visual narrative was anchored by a 550-carat ‚ÄėQueen of Merelani‚Äô tanzanite necklace.
#SudhaReddy #ManishMalhotra #EsquireConnect #MetGala2026

posts.caption:
We all recognise a great catch when we see one. Glen Phillips (@glennphillips236) has been showing them to us aplenty, as did Shreyas Iyer (@shreyasiyer96) when he took flight near the boundary line to pocket a blinder recently. But of late, the ongoing season of the IPL has seen dollies being dropped. And often by the same fielder in the same game, resulting in unexpected losses.
A few months ago, features editor Nitin Sreedhar (@i.moon.child) wrote about the frisson of witnessing spectacular catches and why they captivate long after the moment has passed. ‚ÄúWhen the game gets faster, your fielding has to be quicker,‚ÄĚ IPL veteran Rajat Bhatia told Nitin Sreedhar.
Read the piece at the link in bio.

posts.caption:

Now I‚Äôm trying to take it all to heart as I peruse my next collection
posts.alt_text: Photo by Fashion is Psychology‚ĄĘÔłŹ on March 19, 2025.

posts.caption:
‚ÄúHi Shakaila we‚Äôre xyz producers and we‚Äôd love to make a tv show with you‚ÄĚ I‚Äôve had a handful of those conversations over the years and they quickly go from ūü§© to ūüę©
I used to feel disheartened until I looked around and saw so many examples of people not waiting for permission and instead deciding to get up and make shit happen! So that‚Äôs what I decided to do, turn this decades long dream into a reality! I pulled in every favour I had! Turned my friends into producers and show runners, begged and borrowed and you know what, it turned out pretty damn good if I do say so myself ūüėÖ
This black history month especially, I‚Äôm inspired by the talented Black British women who have shown me that it‚Äôs possible to make a mark on the small screen no matter the limitations!
I absolutely loved makeover shows growing up! But as I got older I saw how they were more brutal than beautiful!
Today, fashion psychology shows us just how deeply our clothes impact our mental health, identity, and confidence.
That‚Äôs why I‚Äôm so excited to share The Wearapy Show my new makeover series that blends fashion psychology, emotional healing and real style transformations.
We help everyday people reclaim their wardrobes after major life shifts: grief, motherhood, identity struggles, and more.
With a team of experts and no judgment, just science-backed guidance and love.
Episodes 1 and 2 launch 26th October on the Fashion is Psychology YouTube Channel. Subscribe now and follow the journey.
#bhm #wearapy #fashionpsychology #makeover

posts.caption:
HUGE NEWS: Good On You has officially rated over 7,000 brands ūüéČ¬†This is a major accomplishment, as even more brands‚ÄĒfrom the small and indie through the large and luxury‚ÄĒare in our Directory.‚Ā†
‚Ā†
What goes into ratings? ūü§Ē A lot, actually: we have a rigorous, comprehensive system analysing up to 1,000 data points across 100 key issues to arrive at our lowest score (‚ÄúWe Avoid‚ÄĚ) to our highest score (‚ÄúGreat‚ÄĚ). Ratings are based solely on a brand‚Äôs transparent, public reporting that we pair with credible third-party indices, certifications, and standards.‚Ā†
‚Ā†
How do we select brands to rate? Well‚Ä¶with your help ūü•Ļ Many of our ratings come through community requests, shared in comments and messages, which helps us understand which brands to prioritise to meet this moment. Additionally, our retail and ecommerce partners along with brands request ratings too, which is a key means to apply change to the industry from within.‚Ā†
‚Ā†
Another item to celebrate here? Of these rated brands, 1,000 have received our two highest scores, ‚ÄúGood‚ÄĚ and ‚ÄúGreat‚ÄĚ. This is very exciting news, representative of how more brands are doing considering their impact on workers, the planet, and animals. (Even if that also means‚Ä¶6000 brands have a lot more to do to reduce their impact.)‚Ā†
‚Ā†
Here‚Äôs to continuing to keep people like you informed while supporting more sustainable brands that are making the difference! Cheers ūü•ā Explore our 7,000 ratings at the link too ‚ú®‚Ā†
‚Ā†
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #SustainableStyle #ConsciousStyle #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion‚Ā†

posts.caption:
Fashion is built on its workers ūüíö‚Ā†
‚Ā†
As @zohrankmamdani shared with @i_d, the ‚Äúfashion industry is made possible by the thousands of workers,‚ÄĚ speaking to the supply chains that go unknown and unseen that keep the industry moving forward. All too often, these workers are underpaid and denied access to autonomy, largely due to brands rewarding those at the top at the expense of those at the bottom.‚Ā†
‚Ā†
When we think of sustainability, we often think of the planet and environments and animals. But, if the business isn‚Äôt sustainable for the people who make our clothes, then it‚Äôs not sustainable at all.‚Ā†
‚Ā†
Swipe for some ideas of how to help workers, then visit the link to learn more about this issue. How are you advocating for garment workers? Share in the comments, and tag a friend as a reminder of the power of people in fashion ūü™°
#Fashion #MetGala #Mamdani #Sustainability #WhoMadeMyClothes

posts.caption:
Is it surprising to anyone that this year‚Äôs Met Gala was a parade of excess? It shouldn‚Äôt have been ūüôÉ‚Ā†
‚Ā†
Fashion has *always* profited from exploitation and, as billionaires prove, wealth inequality in this industry and beyond are exactly why a handful at the top in American and Europe get rich as workers worldwide suffer. If this doesn‚Äôt push you toward ethical fashion, then what will?‚Ā†
‚Ā†
We needn‚Äôt remind you that more sustainable fashion isn‚Äôt just about the environment but a holistic rethinking of the structural issues the industry has long profited off of. More sustainable fashion means all involved are cared for, from those who make your clothes to the communities affected by clothing production ūüíö‚Ā†
‚Ā†
What are your thoughts on this year‚Äôs Met Gala and it‚Äôs proximity to wealth? Is this business as usual? A new low? A searing call to action? It is not the kind of project that one would immediately associate with Sevigny, but that‚Äôs precisely why it also makes sense; she‚Äôll never fit neatly into any box. ‚ÄúMy agents sent it to me, and I was like, ‚ÄėThis is too mainstream. My fans are not going to like it,‚Äô‚ÄĚ Sevigny says, ‚Äúbut I really feel there‚Äôs really something for everybody.‚ÄĚ

posts.caption:
Nate Amos (@hellothisisnate) and Rachel Brown (@heretonetwork), the duo that has been making music as @waterfromyoureyes for almost a decade, are Bazaar's May Music Directors. The two often seem to share similar thoughts‚ÄĒat least when it comes to their musical tastes and references‚ÄĒwhich has resulted in some unexpected covers like Eminem‚Äôs ‚ÄúLose Yourself,‚ÄĚ No Doubt‚Äôs ‚ÄúHella Good,‚ÄĚ and Red Hot Chili Peppers' ‚ÄúScar Tissue." The rest of the playlist has been consciously cobbled together from ‚Äúdifferent periods of music-listening,‚ÄĚ according to Amos. At the link in bio, find a full breakdown of their playlist, which you can stream exclusively now on Apple Music.

posts.caption:
Swiss artist Pipilotti Rist debuted "Ever Is Over All" at the 1997 Venice Biennale, where it won the Premio 2000 award. Twenty years later, it served an undeniable inspiration to @Beyonce‚Äôs ‚ÄúHold Up‚ÄĚ music video. ‚ÄúI consider it an homage by a great artist, and it made me proud,‚ÄĚ Rist shares. On Lemonade‚Äôs 10th anniversary, the Swiss artist talks for the first time about the video and its impact with Bazaar. Tap the link in bio for exclusive details.
Photo: Pipilotti Rist, Still from "Ever Is Over All" (1997). ¬© Pipilotti Rist / ProLitteris, Zurich, DACS, London, ARS, New York Courtesy the artist, Hauser & Wirth and Luhring Augustine

posts.caption: Yes, there is still actual art at the heart of the #MetGala red carpet frenzy. The gala itself has become a polarizing pop-cultural phenomenon and a brand-marketing juggernaut, but it‚Äôs important to remember that there‚Äôs actual artistic intent (and fashion, for that matter) behind the viral event. This year, the @metmuseum‚Äôs latest exhibit begs for a closer examination of our actual human, skin-deep connection to clothes, be they E! News-worthy or not. At the link in our bio, Bazaar‚Äôs @brookebobb spoke to the artist who made the mannequins about the details of the fashion on display‚ÄĒand why the mannequins they chose are the most important part of the message.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Harper‚Äôs BAZAAR on May 06, 2026. May be an image of standing, dress and text that says 'The Mannequins at This Year's Met Exhibit Are Just as Important as as the Clothes BAZAAR'.

posts.caption:
A little polished, a little rebellious, the Voyou Bucket is @givenchy doing ‚Äúeffortless‚ÄĚ in Parisian style.‚Ā†
‚Ā†
Part of the Autumn-Winter 2026 pre-collection, the new silhouette leans into that signature Parisian nonchalance, built around one defining move: a leather drawstring that cinches and reshapes the bag on demand. Designed under Sarah Burton, it mixes clean lines with a subtle biker edge, finished in glossy calf leather and elevated hardware. You can carry it three ways, throw it on with pretty much anything, and make it your own without overthinking it. ‚Ā†
‚Ā†
Available in everything from soft neutrals to standout cobalt, it‚Äôs the kind of bag that adapts to you, not the other way around.

posts.caption:
Too many pockets? Not in POTR‚Äôs world. The brand‚Äôs new DISSECTION collection leans all the way in, turning bags into pocket-packed organizers where utility borders on visual flex. The extra removable pouches almost stop being practical and start feeling like part of the design language.‚Ā†
‚Ā†
From Yoshida & Co.‚Äôs military-minded line, these bags already speak fluent function, but DISSECTION cranks it up. Backpacks, totes, and crossbodies come layered with cargo pockets, mesh compartments, and smartly placed zips so everything has a home. Keys, bottles, even a spare shirt all slot neatly into place, making rummaging a thing of the past. It‚Äôs less ‚Äúbag‚ÄĚ and more neatly engineered command center.

posts.caption:
Fresh off his Tom Ford debut filled with figure-hugging silhouettes, see-through PVC skirts, and lots of leather, the designer is taking that fixation further with a collaboration alongside Jacques Marie Mage. The trio of sunglasses ‚ÄĒ MELCHIOR, BALTHAZAR, and GASPARD ‚ÄĒ each offer a distinct shape and vibe, with the first two even available in leather-wrapped styles.‚Ā†
‚Ā†
As with all @jacquesmariemage pieces, the designs are the result of a 300-step process involving nearly 100 craftspeople, and come packaged in a black leather python-patterned collector‚Äôs case. For JMM founder J√©r√īme Mage, who has recently worked with Jeff Goldblum, Patti Smith, and Yoko Ono, Ackermann stands among ‚Äúthe best creative directors in the world.‚ÄĚ
posts.alt_text: Photo by Highsnobiety Style #HSStyle on May 06, 2026. May be an image of sunglasses, glasses and text.

posts.caption:
Bottega Veneta takes its signature Intrecciato pattern and turns the dial all the way up, reworking the house code with superior precision.‚Ā†
‚Ā†
The new bags play with new patterns in ways that feel both archival and entirely future-facing. It‚Äôs pure craftsmanship but also a reminder that when Bottega Veneta leans into its heritage, it doesn‚Äôt just revisit it; they rewire it.‚Ā†
‚Ā†
The new selection of bags are all available now via Bottega Veneta's online shop. Swipe for a closer look.

posts.caption:
Where heritage meets denim.‚Ā†
‚Ā†
These jeans use a rare double-dyeing process with indigo and safflower, a technique loved in Japan since the Heian period. Woven on an old-school power loom, they capture @studiodartisan signature blend of craftsmanship and traditional Japanese color sensibility.‚Ā†
‚Ā†
Are we feeling the red?

posts.caption:
The bespoke workshop of @timberland ‚ÄúThe Shed‚ÄĚ crafted custom boots for the @publicschoolnyc crew, Dao-Yi Chow and Maxwell Osborne, as they attended the 2026 Met Gala.‚Ā†
‚Ā†
The New Hampshire-based studio is the company‚Äôs exclusive prototyping lab where some of the most ambitious and innovative Timberland models live. The custom Public School NY pairs are composed of Wickett & Craig vegetable-tanned leather, while the 6-Inch Boot‚Äôs body gets exaggerated proportions, and stacked leather midsoles sit on a Vibram Montagna sole. Most noticeably, the toe features a shiny nickel-plated copper guard.‚Ā†
‚Ā†
As of the time of writing, the bespoke design doesn‚Äôt have a planned release, but the special collaboration prompts speculation about a future Timberland partnership in the near future.‚Ā†
‚Ā†
Photo: Public School NY/Timberland‚Ā†

posts.caption:
The quietest looks are always the loudest statements, and Avneet Kaur has known this for a while now. ūüĆł
Draped in the deep, unhurried cool of monochromatic indigo, she doesn‚Äôt chase the moment. She sets the pace for it. Denim-on-denim, lace against leather, coastal light and shadow, every element dialled into a frequency that‚Äôs entirely her own.
In collaboration with @aurelia_womenswear , this isn‚Äôt just a fashion story. It‚Äôs a portrait of a generation that leads with composure, one that finds its power not in the loudest room, but in the stillest one. ‚ú®
The industry moves fast. Avneet doesn‚Äôt rush. And somehow, she‚Äôs always exactly where the moment needs her to be.
Cool, collected, and completely in control, this is the indigo hour. And it belongs to her.

posts.caption:
I really don't understand the obsession with this family. Who writes these contents and captions? Do you at @instylemagazine really believe that we are interested in EVERY MINIMAL movement of this family?
Says everything you need to know about this whole business in this circus. It shows how out of touch the clients, agents, PR strategists, and the whole business have become.
There are so many interesting people out there doing far more significant things and in better outfits, no less? I know you're a lifestyle magazine, but it would really do you some good not to just pick people based on who is a 'money maker ' or has the highest follower count. Or are these just deals with their agents ?
For my part, that's exactly why I haven't bought your magazine in a long time ‚Äď there are far better options out there, and you just keep pushing the same boring people.

A breakout star, Abhay Verma (@verma.abhay_) arrived without the crutches of a star vehicle and is quickly emerging as a commercially viable name with a promising line-up, including Shah Rukh Khan‚Äôs King. He‚Äôs self-assured yet not afraid to be silly with a boyish charm that takes you by surprise. And by his own admission, he must get his flowers!
‚ÄúI‚Äôm already getting some of my favourite flowers: like Shah Rukh sir, my idol, knowing my work and knowing that there‚Äôs a person called Abhay who exists. He fondly hugs me and pats me on my back as a form of ashirwaad whenever I meet him. That‚Äôs one of my favorite flowers.‚ÄĚ
We peek into his world for our April cover, and it‚Äôs a garden of blessings. Read the cover story in the link in the bio.

posts.caption:
Man of the Moment ‚ÄĒ Yashasvi Jaiswal (@yashasvijaiswal28) continues to rise, and we‚Äôre here for every second of it.
From dreaming of centuries in blue to turning those visions into reality and now hitting it out of the park as the opener for Rajasthan Royal (@rajasthanroyals) in IPL, his journey is the very definition of hard work meeting opportunity. Powered as much by an unshakable mindset as by his brilliance on the pitch, our cover star opens up about the drive, discipline, and hunger that fuel his remarkable ascent, ‚ÄúI don‚Äôt know where I‚Äôm going to go, but I want to enjoy the journey.‚ÄĚ
Starting out as a cricketing prodigy, now 24, he‚Äôs all about keeping his head in the game, stopping every now and then, to savour the simple joys of life. And maybe that is the secret sauce to success, one game at a time.
Here‚Äôs raising a toast to the ‚Äėplayer of the match‚Äô- Yashasvi Jaiswal!

posts.caption:
Reality is fine...it has coffee, weekends, and occasionally good weather. But it doesn‚Äôt have a ceiling enchanted to look like the night sky, or a vibranium-powered city that never asked to be discovered, or a camp where Greek gods are real and your summer plans involve actual quests. Fiction has been quietly building our fantasy worlds for decades and we‚Äôre invested.
Here are the 8 fantasy worlds that made us wish, just once, that the magical door was real and ours to open.

Pickleball has officially moved beyond being just a sport, it‚Äôs become part of the lifestyle conversation. And with District Play (@district.play), getting on court feels just as seamless as the rest of your plans: think easy bookings, curated spaces, and zero friction between wanting to play and actually showing up.
What once felt aspirational now feels firmly part of the cultural mood, with scenic courts, social sport, and experience-led leisure shaping pickleball‚Äôs place within India‚Äôs evolving lifestyle landscape. More than just a booking platform, District Play also creates space for something increasingly rare; places to rally, reconnect and unwind somewhere beyond work, home and everything in between.

posts.caption:
Introducing our latest cover star, @katehudson!
It‚Äôs been over 25 years since Kate Hudson earned her first Oscar nomination for her breakout role as Penny Lane in ‚ÄėAlmost Famous,‚Äô and she‚Äôs been shaping the cultural moment ever since. Despite her success‚ÄĒand her roots in Hollywood royalty‚ÄĒshe‚Äôs remained remarkably grounded, continuing to speak about the industry with a genuine sense of gratitude.
‚ÄúI just really want to make movies that people love,‚ÄĚ Hudson tells L‚ÄôOFFICIEL. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs really simple for me. I feel a responsibility as someone that loves this business, and grew up in it, and really wanted to make movies. I hope that I can make movies and shows that people really want to see.‚ÄĚ
That, of course, has always been Hudson‚Äôs strength. Fresh off another Oscar nomination for her role in ‚ÄėSong Sung Blue,‚Äô she‚Äôs now turning her attention back to television with Season 2 of ‚ÄėRunning Point.‚Äô In addition to executive producing the Netflix series, Hudson stars as Isla Gordon, a character loosely inspired by Los Angeles Lakers owner and president Jeanie Buss. While the role has prompted comparisons to Hudson‚Äôs own upbringing, she‚Äôs quick to dismiss that narrative. ‚ÄúJeanie has had to maintain the most important team in sports history, and on top of that, the money and the franchise and everything that goes with it,‚ÄĚ she says. ‚ÄúFor me, we‚Äôre just actors. People are either gonna like what we do or they‚Äôre not.‚ÄĚ
Read our full interview with Hudson about the collaborative nature of film, her creative process, and growing up in the industry at the link in bio.

La storia comincia nel 1954, quando Audrey Hepburn li portava con una marini√®re e un‚Äôeleganza che si fingeva casual. Nel 1962, Jakie Kennedy-Onassis sul molo di Amalfi li trasformava (seppur nella loro versione pi√Ļ morbida e classy) in dichiarazione di stile. Poi il mainstream dei Duemila, il picco, la caduta silenziosa. Fino a Chanel M√©tiers d‚ÄôArt 2021-2022, che li reinterpretava con tutto il peso sartoriale della maison. Le SS26 hanno fatto il resto: da Dries Van Noten ad Akris, da Ralph Lauren alle uscite dai backstage di Parigi e Milano ‚ÄĒ abbinati a kitten heels e canotte bianche, indossati da @BellaHadid, @Emrata, @RealBarbaraPalvin, @HaileyBieber e @SabrinaCarpenter come se fossero la cosa pi√Ļ ovvia del mondo. When we asked Hudson what she still hopes to achieve in her career, she didn‚Äôt point to accolades‚ÄĒjust a desire to keep pursuing roles that scare her, the same approach she brings to everything she does. ‚Ā†
‚Ā†
‚ÄúI enter every experience like it‚Äôs new, like I don‚Äôt know how it‚Äôs going to unfold,‚ÄĚ she explains. ‚ÄúI come in doing my part, which is understanding what makes me feel confident in the role and the character, but I do that so that when I‚Äôm there, I can be flexible, not rigid.‚ÄĚ Read Hudson‚Äôs full interview at the link in our bio. ‚Ā†

posts.caption:
Introducing our latest cover star, @katehudson!
It‚Äôs been over 25 years since Kate Hudson earned her first Oscar nomination for her breakout role as Penny Lane in ‚ÄėAlmost Famous,‚Äô and she‚Äôs been shaping the cultural moment ever since. Despite her success‚ÄĒand her roots in Hollywood royalty‚ÄĒshe‚Äôs remained remarkably grounded, continuing to speak about the industry with a genuine sense of gratitude.
‚ÄúI just really want to make movies that people love,‚ÄĚ Hudson tells L‚ÄôOFFICIEL. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs really simple for me. I feel a responsibility as someone that loves this business, and grew up in it, and really wanted to make movies. I hope that I can make movies and shows that people really want to see.‚ÄĚ
That, of course, has always been Hudson‚Äôs strength. Fresh off another Oscar nomination for her role in ‚ÄėSong Sung Blue,‚Äô she‚Äôs now turning her attention back to television with Season 2 of ‚ÄėRunning Point.‚Äô In addition to executive producing the Netflix series, Hudson stars as Isla Gordon, a character loosely inspired by Los Angeles Lakers owner and president Jeanie Buss. While the role has prompted comparisons to Hudson‚Äôs own upbringing, she‚Äôs quick to dismiss that narrative. ‚ÄúJeanie has had to maintain the most important team in sports history, and on top of that, the money and the franchise and everything that goes with it,‚ÄĚ she says. ‚ÄúFor me, we‚Äôre just actors. People are either gonna like what we do or they‚Äôre not.‚ÄĚ
Read our full interview with Hudson about the collaborative nature of film, her creative process, and growing up in the industry at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Life imitates art, and Monday night‚Äôs Met Gala made that abundantly clear. With the dress code ‚ÄúFashion is Art,‚ÄĚ attendees pulled from everything spanning antiquity‚Äôs most iconic works to more niche, contemporary references. Designers, from @matthieu_blazy and Jonathan Anderson to @pppiccioli, fully embraced the theme through custom looks created for the night‚Äôs attendees, weaving in fine art references alongside archival codes from their respective houses, and turning ‚ÄúFashion is Art‚ÄĚ into wearable interpretation.
Here, we look to some of the art references of the night. @kendalljenner fully committed to the theme, channeling the ‚ÄėWinged Victory of Samothrace‚Äô (190 BCE) in a sculptural look by @Gap Studio, designed by Zac Posen. @roses_are_rosie turned to the 20th century, drawing from Georges Braque‚Äôs ‚ÄėThe Birds‚Äô‚ÄĒa nod to the cubist-turned-expressionist master‚ÄĒthrough a @YSL gown by @anthonyvaccarello, complete with Braque‚Äôs signature bird motif.
@madonna arrived with an entire entourage, reimagining Leonora Carrington‚Äôs ‚ÄėThe Temptation of St. Anthony (Fragment II),‚Äô while Lauren S√°nchez wore @Schiaparelli in a look inspired by John Singer Sargent‚Äôs ‚ÄėPortrait of Madame X.‚Äô With some interpretations more on the nose and others more nuanced, the overarching theme remained clear: fashion as a direct extension of art. For more on the fashion at the Met, visit the link in our bio!
Photos Getty Images
#metgala #metgalaredcarpet #fashionhistory

posts.caption:
For his first Cruise show at @Dior, Jonathan Anderson is heading to Los Angeles‚ÄĒand not quietly. He‚Äôll be the first to stage a runway show at LACMA, turning the museum into Dior‚Äôs latest cinematic set.‚Ā†
‚Ā†
But this isn‚Äôt a random California detour. Dior‚Äôs connection to Hollywood runs deep. Actresses like #MarleneDietrich‚ÄĒwho famously had Dior written into her contracts‚ÄĒblurred the line between character and couture, while #GraceKelly and #RitaHayworth turned those silhouettes into something bigger than fashion.‚Ā†
‚Ā†
And Hollywood did what Hollywood does best‚ÄĒit scaled the fantasy. Dior wasn‚Äôt just dressing women anymore; it was dressing the idea of a star. Film made those looks immortal, exporting them far beyond Paris and embedding them into pop culture.‚Ā†
‚Ā†
Photos Getty Images‚Ā†
‚Ā†
#FashionHistory #DorothyDandridge #NicoleKidman‚Ā†

posts.caption:
Bridgerton isn‚Äôt just a romantic period drama, it‚Äôs one of Netflix‚Äôs most lavish productions. The series reportedly carries a staggering budget of around $168 million, placing it among the most expensive shows created by the streaming giant.
Each episode is estimated to cost around $7 million, funding the show‚Äôs extravagant costumes, grand sets, and cinematic storytelling that recreates Regency-era London. The scale of production is what gives the series its signature opulence and visual richness.
The spending doesn‚Äôt stop at sets and wardrobes. Reports also reveal impressive cast salaries, with some actors and voice talents earning up to $1 million for their roles, reflecting the show‚Äôs massive global success.
From lavish balls to royal romances, Bridgerton proves that luxury storytelling comes with a royal price tag and Netflix is willing to pay for it.
Images: Courtesy of Netflix
#Netflix #Luxurious

posts.caption:
The 2026 Met Gala raised a record-breaking $42 million for the Metropolitan Museum of Art‚Äôs Costume Institute, marking the most successful fundraiser in the event‚Äôs history.
But beyond the fashion, this year‚Äôs gala also reflected Silicon Valley‚Äôs growing influence on luxury culture. With Jeff Bezos and Lauren S√°nchez Bezos reportedly contributing $10 million as lead sponsors, major tech names including Mark Zuckerberg, Sergey Brin, and executives from Meta, Amazon, OpenAI, and Shopify became increasingly visible throughout the night.
While the record-breaking fundraising highlighted the Met Gala‚Äôs expanding financial power, it also sparked conversations around billionaire influence, tech money, and the future direction of fashion‚Äôs biggest night.
#Luxurious #MetGala

posts.caption:
For nearly four decades, Anna Wintour didn‚Äôt just lead Vogue, she reshaped the entire fashion industry.
From transforming magazine covers in the late 1980s to turning the Met Gala into fashion‚Äôs biggest global stage, Wintour built an influence that extended far beyond publishing. Known for her signature bob, dark sunglasses, and uncompromising standards, she became one of the most powerful figures in modern fashion.
Even after stepping down as Vogue‚Äôs editor-in-chief in 2025, Anna Wintour continues to lead as Cond√© Nast‚Äôs global editorial director, proving her impact on fashion and media remains unmatched. (biography.com)
#Luxurious #MetGala

Bhavitha Mandava‚Äôs Met Gala 2026 debut became one of the night‚Äôs most talked-about moments after the Indian model arrived in a denim-inspired Chanel look that sharply contrasted the event‚Äôs usual couture spectacle.
While social media questioned the understated styling, Bhavitha revealed the outfit was a haute couture reinterpretation of the look she wore while opening Chanel‚Äôs M√©tiers d‚ÄôArt show in New York, marking a full-circle moment tied to where she was first discovered.
The seemingly simple ensemble, crafted over 250 hours, reflected Matthieu Blazy‚Äôs vision of elevating everyday dressing through couture craftsmanship, turning what looked like casual denim into one of the gala‚Äôs biggest fashion conversations. (Hindustan Times)

posts.caption:
Audi Tradition has unveiled the recreated Auto Union Lucca, a record-breaking Silver Arrow originally designed in the 1930s.
Built from historical archives and handcrafted over three years, the aerodynamic V16 streamliner pays tribute to the original Rennlimousine that reached 326.9 km/h in 1935, making it one of the fastest road racing cars of its era.
The one-off revival will make its dynamic debut at the 2026 Goodwood Festival of Speed, bringing one of Auto Union‚Äôs most legendary machines back to life nearly 90 years later. (Audi MediaCenter)
@AudiTradition #Audi #Luxurious

Beneath the poise is a life shaped by movement, loss, and emotional inheritance, experiences that continue to inform the way she understands connection and presence.
‚ÄúYou don‚Äôt know what life will throw at you. But as long as you‚Äôre there for each other, that‚Äôs what matters. It‚Äôs about support and presence more than anything else,‚ÄĚ she shares.

posts.caption:
#ManifestCover
There is a quiet power to Rukmini Vasanth (@rukmini_vasanth). One that doesn‚Äôt seek attention, yet holds it effortlessly.
For Manifest‚Äôs May‚ÄďJune cover, she steps into an imaginative world where temple jewellery meets contemporary silhouettes, blurring the lines between memory and modernity. She embodies a woman in transition, grounded, luminous, and yet constantly becoming.
‚ÄúI‚Äôm not very good at describing myself,‚ÄĚ she says, almost in passing.
Yet, in the same breath, her choices reveal a quiet clarity of intent. ‚ÄúI don‚Äôt want to isolate my character. I need to understand the whole world it exists in.‚ÄĚ
Between restraint and reinvention, stillness and movement, she exists in that rare in-between. Not fully defined, not confined. Just on the cusp of a wider sky.

#ManifestCover
At a time when her cinematic world is expanding beyond familiar territories, Rukmini Vasanth (@rukmini_vasanth) remains intentional about the stories she chooses to tell. For her, acting is never about occupying space at the centre of a narrative, but about understanding the emotional ecosystem that surrounds it.
‚ÄúThere was a point where I genuinely didn‚Äôt know where I stood in the industry. Then Sapta Saagaradaache Ello happened, and it changed the way I approached my work. It made me want to keep acting,‚ÄĚ she shares.
Looking ahead, she speaks less about success and more about significance, wanting to be part of films that leave people feeling something long after the credits roll.

‚ÄúIt reminds me of my mother. There‚Äôs something nostalgic about temple jewellery, something understated and classic. It doesn‚Äôt try too hard, but it stays with you,‚ÄĚ she shared.

posts.caption:
Caleb Hearon, a comedian known for his podcast and his stand-up, joined the world of high fashion in ‚ÄúThe Devil Wears Prada 2.‚ÄĚ
Alongside Meryl Streep, Stanley Tucci and Anne Hathaway, he plays an assistant to Miranda Priestly, the demanding, fictional editor of Runway magazine. Hearon‚Äôs character, nicknamed Charlie the Chair because he is not allowed to leave his desk, ‚Äúis stepping into the shoes of Andy Sachs 20 years ago,‚ÄĚ said David Frankel, who directed both films.
Hearon said it‚Äôs a role he never dreamed of booking. ‚ÄúIt‚Äôs ‚ÄėThe Devil Wears Prada‚Äô!" he said. "It is a fashion movie, and we still do not like fat people in this country. I was so pleasantly surprised at how far we‚Äôve come.‚ÄĚ
Hearon is circling the mainstream, and it‚Äôs obvious to those in his orbit. Regardless of his accomplishments, though, he still sees himself as the Caleb who was born and raised in rural Missouri. His Midwestern earnestness and affability draw people to him, but does his podcast-friendly personality translate into Hollywood stardom? Tap the link in our bio to read the full profile of @calebsaysthings. Photo by @voyeur1
