posts.caption:
When ‚ÄúFashion Is Art,‚ÄĚ the dress code for the Met Gala on Monday night, was announced, it seemed like a risk. After all, fashion has been trying for decades to prove its bona fides as an equal of the other fine arts at the Metropolitan Museum of Art.
The gala has increasingly been walking the line between elegant and absurd, as guests attempt to one-up one another in the attention economy. Giving celebrities ‚Äúart‚ÄĚ as a theme could have inspired a host of old masters and Impressionism cosplay rather than any sort of consideration about what it actually means in fashion terms.
See Heidi Klum, who walked as a Grecian or Roman statue. Or Ben Platt in a Tanner Fletcher suit that evoked Georges Seurat‚Äôs ‚ÄúA Sunday Afternoon on the Island of La Grande Jatte.‚ÄĚ Or Anne Hathaway, who had a Grecian figure appliqu√©d onto the skirt of her Michael Kors gown.
More than just flirt with costume, the Met Gala‚Äôs dress code also ‚Äúacted as a reminder of the distinction between fashion and art,‚ÄĚ wrote Vanessa Friedman, chief fashion critic for The New York Times. In her analysis, @vvfriedman examines guests‚Äô interpretations of the dress code. Tap the link in our bio to read. Photo by @vnina
posts.alt_text: Photo by New York Times Fashion & Style on May 06, 2026. May be an image of one or more people, hair, makeup, dress, magazine and text that says 'Was It Art? Was It Fashion? Was It Good?'.

posts.caption:
Long after the photographers had left the green cobblestone carpet of the Met Gala, celebrities filed into after-parties across New York City. At Boom, at the top of the Standard in the Meatpacking District of Manhattan, the designer Gabriela Hearst co-hosted a party with the actress Grace Gummer. Guests like ASAP Rocky and Lindsey Vonn mingled while Questlove spun vintage Chaka Khan.
Across town, the movie director Baz Luhrmann and his wife, the costume designer Catherine Martin, hosted a celebration at their East Village bar, Monsieur. At the Lower East Side nightclub the Box, scantily dressed dancers in sky-high heels shimmied beneath a disco ball before the D.J. Diplo arrived for his set.
The New York Times reporters @jacobbernsteinnyc and @smbharh14 caught up with the stars as they hit the after-parties across the city. Tap the link in our bio to get a look at who was out and what they wore. Photos by @rebeccasmeyne, @amirbangs and @vnina
posts.alt_text: Photo by New York Times Fashion & Style on May 05, 2026. May be an image of magazine, dinner jacket and text that says 'Inside the Met Gala After-Parties After-P'.

posts.caption:
The Met Gala steps are a stage where celebrities strive not merely to look pretty, but to create indelible, avant-garde fashion moments. The most memorable looks tend to commit to the often abstract theme and take risks not typically seen on award show red carpets. Sometimes that works; other times it ends in disaster.
This year, guests stepped onto a mossy-looking, trompe l‚Äôoeil cobblestone carpet surrounded by a backdrop that recalled a Monet canvas. It was fitting for the evening‚Äôs brief: ‚ÄúFashion is art.‚ÄĚ It may be a bit on the nose for an event whose raison d‚Äô√™tre is to raise money for the Metropolitan Museum of Art‚Äôs fashion-focused Costume Institute.
Of all of the looks from the Met Gala, presented in no particular order, these were our unequivocal favorites. Tap the link in our bio to see all 15 looks. Photos by @vnina and @amirbangs

posts.caption:
At this year‚Äôs Met Gala, some famous public figures were hidden behind masks, blindfolds, headpieces and makeup that created the illusion of a marble statue or an aging man. You might not know it to look at them, but the night was a celebration of the body.
Guests including Rihanna and ASAP Rocky, Doja Cat, Beyonc√© and her daughter Blue Ivy showed out on Monday night, wearing outfits that riffed on the idea of ‚Äúthe dressed body,‚ÄĚ the focus of the new fashion exhibition. That included corsets, foam, barely-there Saint Laurent gowns and different styles of flowers.
Dressed as a marble statue was Heidi Klum, wearing a look inspired by the Veiled Vestal, a 19th-century sculpture by Raffaelle Monti. She planned the look for three months and it took her five hours to get ready, she said, adding that it was the best her body had felt at a Met Gala. ‚ÄúFor once, my feet don‚Äôt hurt,‚ÄĚ she said, wiggling her marble-like toes in flat sandals.
The New York Times reporter @s_evangelina and the photographer @landonnordeman caught up with guests at the gala‚Äôs cocktail party. Tap the link in our bio to step inside the fete and read more about how guests spent their night at the museum.

posts.caption:
Remember that viral #Loewe tomato bag? (Shoutout @conndo) Well, it seems #JonathanAnderson‚Äôs love for fruit and vegetables has left a lasting impression on Loewe‚Äôs designs. Their latest ¬£4,000 leather artichoke bag makes you wonder, why are they so obsessed with fruit and vegetables?
These items aren‚Äôt just gimmicks; they‚Äôre demonstrations of Loewe‚Äôs craftsmanship, requiring intricate pattern cutting, moulding, and a high level of attention to detail. It also pays homage to the brand‚Äôs Spanish roots, drawing on a Mediterranean aesthetic of fresh produce and wellness. It aligns with a more relaxed, lifestyle-driven idea of luxury.
There‚Äôs also a sense of surrealism, where ordinary objects are elevated. That‚Äôs why these pieces feel more like wearable art than conventional accessories. Would you buy your 5 a day from @loewe ?

posts.caption:
In the early 2010s, #Topshop was one of the hottest brands to wear to the #MetGala, dressing all the rising ‚ÄúIt girls.‚ÄĚ Over a decade later, fast fashion is borrowing more than ever from the credibility and visibility of luxury spaces like the Met, as a powerful marketing platform.
When a celebrity wears something like Zara by #JohnGalliano or Gap by #ZacPosen, it blurs the line between mass and luxury. Obviously, these dresses aren‚Äôt coming from the same places as the jeans and T-shirts we buy in stores, but they still function as cultural symbols and marketing tools.
Whether celebrities should wear these brands depends on what you think the Met Gala is meant to represent. On one hand, it reflects the current reality of fashion. On the other, it risks turning the event into a marketing vehicle for mass retailers and diluting the focus on craftsmanship and artistry that traditionally defined the gala.
What do you think?

posts.caption:
#PhilipTreacy‚Äôs ship hat is probably one of the most beautiful pieces of millinery I‚Äôve ever seen, and #Madonna wearing it to the 2026 #MetGala was the perfect excuse to talk about it again.
Philip made the hat for Isabella Blow in 1994. He was inspired by the 18th-century Coiffure √† la Belle-Poule, a nautical hairstyle that featured a model ship sailing atop a sea of thick, sculpted hair.
In 1995, #MichaelJackson reportedly offered $25,000 for the hat; however, Philip refused as he only had one. He said ‚Äúeven though I love Michael Jackson, I just didn‚Äôt want it going off to Neverland.‚ÄĚ The piece went on to become an iconic moment in fashion history. It inspired designers like #JeanPaulGaultier, who revived the headpiece again for his 2025 couture collection in collaboration with Ludovic de Saint Sernin
Image courtesy of Gallica Biblioth√®que Nationale de France / Kieran Doherty / Ram Shergill / Ricardo Gomes

posts.caption:
Last weekend, I double-featured The Devil Wears Prada with its freshly minted sequel. Two decades later, the sleek, cutthroat halls of Runway magazine are back, and Miranda Priestly is once again staring down the barrel of a shifting culture. Watching the two films back-to-back proved to be visceral in how culture stubbornly refuses to compromise.
Right now, if you spend any time observing Silicon Valley‚Äôs tech elite, there is a frantic, almost desperate obsession with one word: Taste.
The tech world has built the machines, they have trained the models, and now they are staring at the void, realising that algorithmic optimisation is not the same thing as art. They want culture and nuance.
In an era drowning in corporate-dictated digital slop and automated mediocrity, this sequel is a middle finger to the machine. It is fiercely, unapologetically anti-AI, and carries a necessary cultural correction. It prompts us to look around and ask who or what is choosing what we consume. Are we surrendering our aesthetics to the predictable, frictionless churn of artificial intelligence, or are we demanding the grit, the friction, and the divine arrogance of human curation?
Opinion by shalakakulkarni | Views expressed by the author are their own.
Read more at www.shethepeople.tv.
#devilwearsprada #mirandapriestly Andy sachs anne Hathaway Nigel Stanley tucci meryl streep Miranda priestly Emily blunt Emily charlton

posts.caption:
Coimbatore's Arunachalam Muruganantham, recognised as the inventor of a low-cost sanitary pad-making machine in India, has been nominated for the Nobel Peace Prize 2026. His machines, which can make sanitary pads for less than a third of the cost of commercial pads, have been installed in 23 of the 28 states of India in rural areas. He is reportedly planning to expand the production of these machines to 106 countries.
Muruganantham is credited with mobilising grassroots women by making hygienic sanitary products more accessible and raising awareness around menstruation. His story inspired the 2018 film Pad Man, starring Akshay Kumar and Radhika Apte.
Read more at www.shethepeople.tv

posts.caption:
Not every bridal look is about tradition in its expected form.
Sometimes, it‚Äôs about rewriting it entirely. ‚ú®
Think of your wedding wardrobe not just as something you wear‚Ä¶ but as a reflection of personality, where colour, craft, and individuality come together without rules.
Especially when it‚Äôs a Masaba Gupta bride‚Ä¶ every look feels playful yet powerful, rooted in culture while unapologetically modern.
Because the outfit may catch your eye‚Ä¶
but it‚Äôs the confidence behind it that makes it unforgettable.
The prints that instantly stand apart.
The unexpected colour palettes that feel bold yet effortless.
The drapes styled with a touch of rebellion.
The jewellery layered with intention, not convention.
It‚Äôs never about fitting into one bridal mould‚Ä¶
It‚Äôs about creating a look that feels entirely your own. ūüíę
Because the most memorable brides aren‚Äôt the ones who follow every tradition exactly as it is‚Ä¶
They‚Äôre the ones who wear their individuality beautifully.
In 2026, bridal fashion feels less about perfection and more about personality, embracing looks that are expressive, experimental, and deeply personal.
So if your bridal look told a story‚Ä¶ would it be timelessly classic or beautifully unconventional? ūü§ć

posts.caption:
Some moments don‚Äôt arrive all at once‚Ä¶ they unfold in glances, in laughter, in the quiet magic of being side by side. ‚ú®
Under a canopy that felt almost ethereal, with petals scattered like fragments of a dream, this was more than just a ritual, it was a moment suspended between joy and forever. The kind where everything around fades just enough for two people to truly see each other.
There was something so effortless in the way they stood together‚Ä¶ his arm wrapped around her like it had always belonged there, her eyes lifting toward him with a softness that said everything words couldn‚Äôt. Playful, certain, completely at ease, like love had already found its rhythm between them.
And then, in the middle of it all, came those fleeting little exchanges, the adjusting of garlands, the almost-smiles, the shared anticipation, turning tradition into something deeply personal. Not just a ceremony, but a memory in the making.
Because sometimes, it‚Äôs not the grand gestures that stay with you‚Ä¶ it‚Äôs the way someone looks at you when the world is watching, and yet, it feels like it‚Äôs just the two of you. ‚ú®

posts.caption:
Not every moment in a wedding is just about how it unfolds.
Some carry meaning in the way they‚Äôre felt‚Ä¶ quietly, yet profoundly. ‚ú®
In the moment of the varmala, that meaning becomes unmistakable. It‚Äôs not just about garlands being exchanged‚Ä¶ it‚Äôs about two people choosing each other, right there, in the presence of everyone who matters.
Beyond the laughter, the playful lifts, and the cheering crowd‚Ä¶ there are frames that feel suspended in time. Where everything else softens, and for a second, it‚Äôs just them.
A glance held a little longer.
A smile that arrives without trying.
Arms reaching, not just to place a varmala‚Ä¶ but to close a distance that no longer exists.
Some frames feel lighthearted, filled with mischief and movement. Others feel still‚Ä¶ almost sacred, like the world decided to pause and witness something real.
The way the bride leans in, the way the groom steadies the moment‚Ä¶ it‚Äôs never just choreography. It‚Äôs instinct. It‚Äôs emotion finding its way through tradition.
And while every wedding has its own energy, these moments remain universal. Because no matter the setting, the d√©cor, or the scale‚Ä¶ this is where it all becomes real.
Think of these frames as more than just photographs‚Ä¶ they are pauses turned into memories. The kind you don‚Äôt just see, but feel.
Because long after the celebrations fade‚Ä¶ this is the moment that stays. ūüíę
Which varmala frame felt like forever to you? ‚ú®

posts.caption:
Fashion styling is a job. Not a hobby. Not a passion project. Not something people do on the side because they love clothes.
It is a profession that takes years to build. The eye. The contacts. The knowledge of how the industry actually works across every sector, every brief, every type of client. That does not come from loving fashion. It comes from years of showing up, doing the work and figuring it out mostly alone.
And yet it is still one of the most undervalued roles in the creative industry.
Stylists are asked to work for free. For credit. For exposure. For a rate that does not come close to reflecting the skill, the hours or the experience involved. And somewhere along the way the industry decided that was normal. That stylists should be grateful for the opportunity. That loving what you do means you should not expect to be paid properly for it.
We are past that.
Behind every iconic image, every red carpet moment, every campaign that stopped you mid-scroll, there was a stylist. Building the looks, interpreting the brief, managing the pressure, absorbing the chaos so nobody else had to. Often without a credit. Sometimes without a proper rate.
That changes when stylists know their worth, charge it without apology and stop accepting the jobs that treat their career like a favour.
‚ě°ÔłŹ‚ě°ÔłŹ If you are a working fashion stylist who is ready to do exactly that, comment STUDIO and we will send you the free trial link to The Studio of Stylist Elixir. Rate guides. Contracts. Portfolio tools. Masterclasses every Monday. Everything you need to build a career that reflects what you are actually worth.
Save this. Send it to a stylist who needs to hear it.

posts.caption: If something needs such in-depth analysis to be understood than the assignment was a failure! It‚Äôs not a dissertation on the politics of 17th century France. It‚Äôs CLOTHING! It‚Äôs a fashion show and since fashion shows have been able to be seen by millions and consumed in photos and video for decades, those who are not in the loop to see the clothes for real are only left with what we see and to dare to say that just because a design has ‚ÄúReferences‚ÄĚ or inner history negates it from being judged as what it simply LOOKS LIKE, is insultingly arrogant, something that takes 4,000 hours of the most intricate and elaborate technique‚Ä¶ CAN STILL LOOK HORRID! All that time does not acquit it of its ugliness or stupidity of design! When all that history and back story sgd references WORK IN CONJUNCTION with the clothes to crest something magical (Blazy/Chanel) then the framework of all that in depth research and application of history is breathtaking, when it‚Äôs like JWA/Dior, it just comes across as over focus on ideas that then translate into nothing more than dissertations on a thought than the true purpose of what is happening‚Ä¶ MAKING BEAUTIFUL CLOTHES! And for you to suggest that unless you‚Äôre erudite and informed of history that you shouldn‚Äôt comment is the height of condescension! Anyone can see! And no matter how layered the meaning, if it looks like all that time taken to produce garbage still looks like GARBAGE, it changes not its beauty because you have the knowing of the thought processes and HISTOIRE D‚ÄôMAISON behind you! It‚Äôs pretentious and asinine

posts.caption:
There are things about being a fashion stylist that nobody outside the job will fully understand.
The hours spent looking for one specific thing. The cab ride home with eight bags. The returns that take longer than the shoot. The cry in the back of the taxi after a job that nobody knew was almost a disaster.
The pride of a pinned hem nobody will ever see. The PR email that reads friendly but is not. The way a stylist‚Äôs brain does not switch off, even at a dinner party.
This is the post for the stylists who recognise themselves in this. Save it. Send it to the friend
who will laugh. Send it to the assistant who is just starting and is wondering if it is supposed to
feel like this. It is.
The career looks glamorous and feels like work. Both are true. Anyone who says it is only one is wrong.
Comment STYLIST to be in a room with stylists who get it.
With love, Em x

posts.caption:
Fashion stylists are done explaining what they do.
Explaining what you actually do all day.
Explaining why the rate is what it is.
Explaining that prep is the work.
Explaining that a press credit is not a paycheque. Explaining that you are not available for this one, and no, you do not need to suggest someone else.
The explaining is exhausting. It is also the reason the industry stays the way it is. The more we explain, the more the next generation grows up thinking the work needs explaining at all.
This is the post for the stylists who are done. Done apologising for the rate. Done justifying the prep. Done turning visibility into compensation. Done shrinking the scope of the job to make it easier for someone else to understand.
The work is the work. The rate is the rate. The job is real and skilled and full and earned.
Comment STYLIST to find out how we can help. ūüíö

posts.caption:
Working for credit is one of the biggest hidden costs in a styling career.
Every shoot taken for exposure is a shoot that is not being paid for. Every ‚Äúgreat for your portfolio‚ÄĚ is a polite way of asking for free labour. The industry runs on the assumption that stylists will keep saying yes because the next job might be the one that pays. Most of the time it isn‚Äôt.
Getting out of the credit cycle is not about turning more work down. It is about knowing how to spot the unpaid ask before saying yes, and how to push back without losing the relationship.
That is the conversation we have on the 1:1 calls. Where you are now. What is paying. What is not. And the script that gets you out of the credit cycle and into work that actually funds your career.
Book a 1:1 call: stylistelixir.com
With love, Em x‚Äč‚Äč‚Äč‚Äč‚Äč‚Äč‚Äč‚Äč‚Äč‚Äč‚Äč‚Äč‚Äč‚Äč‚Äč‚Äč

posts.caption:
The 2026 Met Gala delivered. The looks have been ranked, posted, screenshotted, judged.
This is not a round-up. Stylist Elixir is not about critique other stylists. It is about the work behind the work.
The stylists who built the looks. The assistants who carried the bags. The tailors who finished
the seams in hotel hallways. The glam teams who synced every detail. The PRs who held the
timeline together. The couriers who got the pieces there. The producers who made the
impossible run on time.
Most of them will not get tagged. Most of them will not be named in a caption. That does not make their work less real. The fittings still happened. The samples still got there. The look still landed.
Recognition should not be a luxury. It should reflect the effort that built the moment. To everyone who worked on this year‚Äôs gala, in front of the camera or behind it. We see you.
And to the talent and the stylists who tagged their teams properly today. Thank you. The credit does not just say who. It says this work was collaborative. That nothing on a red carpet builds itself.

posts.caption:
Every bar calendar now seems to have a guest shift on it.
What began as bartender swaps has become India‚Äôs new cocktail flex: visiting mixologists, limited-edition menus, global bar names and alcohol brands finding a smoother way into the craft conversation.
Awards gave bartenders star power. Brands found a marketing format that feels less like a hard sell. Drinkers got novelty without abandoning the comfort of a familiar bar.
The catch is obvious. When every night is programmed to feel special, special starts to feel scheduled. A bar can become known for hosting everyone else before it becomes known for itself.
Novelty brings people in. Comfort brings them back.

posts.caption:
At the Met Gala 2026, fashion has been framed as art, and celebrities have showcased clothing as material, labour, and object.
Karan Johar (@karanjohar) in Manish Malhotra (@manishmalhotra05) treats the body as canvas, with painted and embroidered surfaces drawing from Raja Ravi Varma, while Malhotra‚Äôs own bandhgala foregrounds labour, displaying the process as a part of the final piece.
Siblings Gauravi Kumari (@gauravikumari) and Padmanabh Singh in Prabal Gurung (@pachojaipur) have built directly from archive and atelier. Gauravi‚Äôs look incorporates her grandmother, Maharani Gayatri Devi‚Äôs chiffon sari into a couture structure that preserves its drape, while Padmanabh‚Äôs Phulghar coat, developed over 600+ hours in velvet with aari, zardozi, dabka and resham work, turns Rajasthani craft into surface and object.
Natasha Poonawalla (@natasha.poonawalla) in Dolce & Gabbana √ó Marc Quinn shifts fashion into sculpture, centring an orchid form as the primary object, with the body acting as its base.
By drawing on craft traditions, art-historical references, and material-led construction, these celebrities have positioned their presence as part of a larger exchange where fashion operates as a legitimate artistic language on an international stage. They‚Äôre not merely wearers of couture.
#met2026

posts.caption:
Reliance Retail‚Äôs beauty ambitions are getting harder to miss. The company has acquired Anomaly, the haircare brand co-founded by Priyanka Chopra Jonas, along with its trademarks, brand assets and digital properties, giving it full ownership of the brand‚Äôs intellectual property and digital ecosystem.
Financial terms of the deal have not been disclosed. Chopra Jonas is expected to remain associated with the brand as creative director, with a focus on product development, innovation and brand strategy.
Launched in the US in 2021, Anomaly entered India the following year through Nykaa. But in an increasingly crowded beauty market, the brand failed to become a category-defining brand, proving celebrity beauty is no longer enough on its own.
The acquisition now moves the brand into Reliance Retail‚Äôs growing beauty system, which include Tira and Sephora India. Under Reliance, the bet is that scale, shelf space and visibility can do what a celebrity alone could not.
For Reliance, Anomaly is another celebrity-backed brand in the portfolio. But the real acquisition is influence over the shelf, the platform and the consumer journey.
The celebrity gets the headline. The real business happens at the shelf.

@dairyboy by @paigelorenze has seriously mastered the art of a creative campaign. Sun-faded textures, animal motifs, worn-in textiles, Scottish plaids‚Ä¶ it all feels intentionally nostalgic without slipping fully into costume.
The Farm Friends campaign leans into the imperfections of country life‚ÄĒlivestock, greenery, natural lighting, slightly undone styling‚ÄĒand ends up feeling far more lived-in than the overly polished cottagecore aesthetic that‚Äôs flooded the internet for the past few years. Makes me reconsider city life.

posts.caption:
I know everyone is still locked into Met Gala discourse, but we can‚Äôt forget to wish a happy belated birthday to the late Audrey Hepburn.
Hepburn completely shifted the fashion landscape of the 1950s and 60s away from excess and toward something cleaner, sharper, and infinitely more timeless. Through her partnership with Hubert de Givenchy, she helped define a new kind of elegance built on simplicity, tailoring, and restraint‚ÄĒmost famously in Breakfast at Tiffany‚Äôs and that iconic black dress.
What made her style endure wasn‚Äôt trend-chasing. It was consistency. She understood personal style before the internet turned it into content.
She‚Äôll always be our girl.
#AudreyHepburn #Givenchy #FashionHistory #BreakfastAtTiffanys #FashionFilm

Rihanna, SZA, Beyonc√©, Madonna, Anok Yai, Hunter Schafer, and Miles Chamley-Watson all delivered‚ÄĒand I was especially relieved to see Maison Margiela back on the carpet, plus a few looks that actually leaned into costume and performance instead of playing it safe.

posts.caption:
Initial read on the 2026 Met Gala is giving a disconnect between concept and execution. A theme as expansive as ‚ÄúCostume Art‚ÄĚ requires clarity of interpretation‚ÄĒand so far, that clarity feels uneven.
We got off to a bit of a rough start. Let‚Äôs be real: attendees aren‚Äôt really dressing on theme. Yes, there are referential looks (and those matter), but referencing something isn‚Äôt the same as executing it well. Right now, it‚Äôs reading more underwhelming than intentional.
That said, there are highlights. Robert Wun is consistently delivering, and Beyonc√© is officially back after a decade, attending with Blue Ivy Carter, both in custom Olivier Rousteing. The moment we‚Äôve all been waiting for.
Some strong moments‚ÄĒjust not enough of them yet. More coverage soon.
#MetGala #CostumeArt #FashionIsArt #TheCitizensPoste #Beyonc√©

posts.caption:
The Met Gala is officially live in NYC‚ÄĒand the 2026 theme, ‚ÄúCostume Art,‚ÄĚ is‚Ä¶ being interpreted. But we do have some favorites so far. With the focus on the body as canvas, some looks are leaning more sculptural than others. Robert Wun (notably on Lisa and Naomi Osaka) is delivering strong structure and veiling, while Schiaparelli under Daniel Roseberry is doing its usual anatomical surrealism.
Bur√ß Aykol is also exploring the body more directly through drapery, and Heidi Klum (as always) committed to a full sculpture moment.
Overall: a few clear interpretations, a lot of safe ones. Who‚Äôs standing out to you?

posts.caption:
Happy Met Gala Monday to those who celebrate‚ÄĒand if you don‚Äôt, consider this your invitation. Tonight is one of the few moments where fashion fully takes center stage. The Met Gala isn‚Äôt just a red carpet; it‚Äôs a funding mechanism for the Costume Institute, a form of preservation, and one of the only spaces where fashion is treated with the same cultural weight as art.
And with that comes nuance. You can love the spectacle‚ÄĒthe looks, the drama, the commentary‚ÄĒwhile also questioning the structure, the exclusivity, and what it represents. Both can exist at once.
Enjoy it. Analyze it. Have opinions. And as always, stay tuned for all of The Citizen‚Äôs Poste‚Äôs coverage tonight.
#MetGala #FashionCulture #RedCarpet #MetGala2026 #CostumeInstitute

posts.caption: Warning to anyone wanting to buy with Vestiaire‚Ä¶. Don‚Äôt do it. Bought a LV purse but instantly questioned authenticity (literally as soon as I purchased it) went straight on chat about this. They kindly offered to verify it. The purse came to me and it definitely appeared fake to me‚Ä¶. No heat stamp inside, no serial number, very thin cheap dust bag. Hardware not typical of LV. Sent it off for verification. Waited a few days and today I get a notification to say it‚Äôs been verified and is authentic‚Ä¶. ūüęĘ I do not believe it for a second. Go on to chat to discuss, they say it‚Äôs got a serial number (seller also told me no serial number as well as when I looked myself) so I asked where the serial number is and could I have a picture‚Ä¶ bearing in mind now they have it they are refusing to refund me and they are listing it to be resold. They‚Äôve said they can‚Äôt send me a picture and can‚Äôt provide me where it is. Which all makes no sense to me whatsoever. I‚Äôve reminded them that me asking for that serial number is me asking for that of my property. I don‚Äôt believe it‚Äôs real and therefore don‚Äôt wish to keep it but it is mine. Asked to speak to a manager and denied. I mean I may get a refund if it sells in 2yrs? What if it doesn‚Äôt sell at all? I imagine that I won‚Äôt see that listing and really they will do anything with it because they have it in their possession even though it‚Äôs mine?? Absolutely vile company. I am a small buyer I‚Äôve not spent thousands. I know me not purchasing will make no difference really but I did want to warn others. I will never use them again, I‚Äôm going to make a claim on PayPal and leave a trust pilot review‚Ä¶. It‚Äôs a good job I guess that it wasn‚Äôt something worth hundreds or thousands. Very very bad company.

posts.caption:
A haunting Delhi bungalow made of thread, a panel of fractured soil and clay from an artist‚Äôs home in Tirunelveli in Tamil Nadu, a papier-m√Ęch√© Ladakhi township, a delicate garden that defies gravity and site-specific bamboo scaffolding that‚Äôs more organic than overwhelming. After a seven-year absence, India‚Äôs return to the Venice Biennale(@labiennale) is not big on spectacle or packed with master/blue-chip artists.
Curated by Amin Jaffer(@aminjaffer_curator), director of the Al Thani Collection(@al.thani.collection) and formerly of the Victoria and Albert Museum(@vamuseum), the exhibit titled Geographies of Distance: remembering home is a succinct showcase of five seminal works by five contemporary Indian artists‚ÄĒSumakshi Singh(@sumakshisingh), Asim Waqif(@asimwaqif), Alwar Balasubramaniam (Bala), Skarma Sonam Tashi(@skarma_sonam_tashi), and Ranjani Shettar.
It signals a country quiet in its confidence, rooted in memory and materiality yet deeply contemporary, even forward-thinking in its outlook. And it‚Äôs bang on the theme: ‚ÄėIn Minor Keys‚Äô, the title of the 61st International Art Exhibition at La Biennale di Venezia, is a sort of quiet resistance against the dominant.
@shahnazsiganporia in conversation with Jaffer at the link in bio.
Photography: @joe.habben @ave_zz

posts.caption:
MET Gala: Beauty‚Äôs Starring Role
This year the MET Gala explored fashion as art and beauty took up a starring role. The most experimental and our favourite from the night? Anok Yai‚Äôs face, painted in gold with 3D tear drops glistening down referencing the iconic Black Madonna sculpture. Another favourite included Bad Bunny‚Äôs take on the ‚Äúageing body‚ÄĚ. In the age of baby botox, collagen shots and facelifts, he walked up the MET stairs with a cane in tow and an aged-prosthetic face, ‚Äú53 years‚ÄĚ in the making.
Reliance Retail Acquires Priyanka Chopra‚Äôs Anomaly
Lifestyle conglomerate Reliance Retail has acquired actor Priyanka Chopra Jonas‚Äôs global haircare brand, Anomaly. First launched in 2021, Anomaly has established itself in the global market for its clean and vegan formulations.
Footnote: Birkenstock‚Äôs Nail Paint Venture
The 250-year old German footwear company, Birkenstock, which is popular for its orthopedic-focused sandals with a specialised footbed design has launched a glossy range of five vegan, plant-based nail paints crafted to match the colour palette of its Arizona slippers. These polishes, priced at $12 each, are sold alongside a clear base and a top coat.
Maison Margiela‚Äôs Couture Fragrances
Drawing inspiration from the world of haute couture, the L‚ÄôOr√©al Group-owned Maison Margiela has recently unveiled a new lineup of artisanal haute fragrances. Titled Scentsorium, the six scents were three years in the making by the luxury maison‚Äôs former creative head John Galliano and have been released as an aromatic equivalent of its couture clothing.
SK-II Debuts in India
The luxe legacy beauty label SK-II has finally debuted in the Indian market courtesy LUXASIA, the omnichannel beauty distributor.
Attended by Hollywood actor and global brand ambassador Anna Sawai, the immersive launch event last month further noted a Magic Scan‚ÄĒthe AI driven analysis for attendees, exploring skin-age and other concerns.

Using the language of craft, ‚ÄúThe Right to Be Seen: Reframing the Passport Photo in Craft‚ÄĚ ‚Äď idea, concept, and direction by The Voice of Fashion; curated by Juhi Pandey for Journeying Across The Himalayas 2025 ‚Äďis a series built around the concept of the unfiltered identity photograph.
Working with artisan communities across the Indian Himalayan region, the exhibition shifts focus from craft to maker. Each portrait emerges as an interpretation of the self, shaped through an intimate engagement with material and practice.
As The Himalayan Knot travels across continents, presented by the Friday Sari Project Curated Store and Showcase at London Craft Week, a collection of portraits of Raziya Bano ‚Äď an artisan from Kargil, India ‚Äď will be on display. Catch it from 12‚Äď17 May 2026.

posts.caption:
Hi, I‚Äôm Veronica ūüĎčūüŹľ a sewing pattern designer based in Melbourne, Australia.
I create patterns, tools and resources that help you sew with understanding.
My work focuses on how fabrics behave, how garments are constructed, and how sewing connects us to the history of what we wear.
Sewing Patterns
My patterns are designed for versatility, wearability and fun. They include the Hazel, Freya, Odette, Audrey, Hera, Cordelia and the Sleeve Studio ‚ÄĒ each combining comfort with thoughtful design details. You‚Äôll also find The Noelle, a free beginner-friendly pattern that introduces my approach to fit and construction.
The Paper Doll
A print-at-home paper doll designed to help you visualise and play with my sewing patterns. You can cut it out and colour it yourself, or use the pre-filled versions featuring my fabric prints and colour palettes. The set includes curvier model options and three skin tones to choose from.
The Conscious Consumer eBook
A practical guide to fabrics, sustainability and sewing with intention. It helps you understand fibre origins, weave structures and how to make long-lasting choices when you sew or buy clothes.
Digital Sewing Tools
Two paid calculators designed for precision:
‚ÄĘ Shirring Calculator ‚Äď works out fabric shrinkage and elastic thread length.
‚ÄĘ GSM Calculator ‚Äď estimates fabric weight and drape.
Available individually or as a discounted bundle.
My PDF sewing patterns are stocked digitally through @fabricgodmother , @spotlightstores , @thefoldline , @joans.finefabric and @thedrapersdaughter .
Fabric and Accessories
You can shop my fabric designs on @spoonflower and find my illustrated phone cases at @thedairy .
Everything I create is designed to help you sew with confidence, understanding and creativity.
For all enquires email us at hello@veronicatuckerthelabel.com

posts.caption:
A few days ago I posted about Mariano Fortuny and promised a full post on Henriette, because she deserved the spotlight rather than the margin.
This is that post.
Everything here traces to a single document‚Ä¶a patent filed in Paris on 10 June 1909, held at the Marciana Library in Venice. Mariano filed it under his name because he was present and the deadline required it. Then he wrote a note on the same document, in his own handwriting, naming Henriette Negrin as the inventor.
That note sat in the archive for 112 years but the V&A only updated their catalogue labels in 2021.
Mariano‚Äôs acknowledgement was there from the beginning‚Ä¶it just took the institution a century to follow it.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Veronica Tucker the Label on May 04, 2026. May be an image of poster, magazine and text that says 'The Goddess TheGoddessProject Project The mind behind Fortuny Her name was Henriette Negrin. Here is what Hereiswhatsheactuallydid. she actually did. veronica veronicatucker tucker'.

posts.caption:
Yesterday I posted about the Indus Valley, where cotton was first threaded through copper beads in 6000 BCE, where the draped rectangle became the primary garment of one of the largest civilisations in the ancient world.
That logic travelled west through Mesopotamia, Egypt and Greece. In 1909 it arrived in a palazzo on the Grand Canal in the hands of a man who rowed himself across the Venetian lagoon every morning to make gowns that could not be dated.
Mariano Fortuny called himself a painter but else called him the Magician of Venice. He was severely allergic to horses, obsessed with electricity and spent his life reaching for a father he lost at three years old.
Peggy Guggenheim wore the same Delphos for decades. Isadora Duncan wore it to revive Classical Greek dance. Marcel Proust used it as a symbol of desire, memory, and lost time throughout one of the greatest novels ever written.
The Met @metmuseum chose it to announce this year‚Äôs theme. Fashion is Art and Fortuny already knew.
He was reaching for ancient Greece but the tradition he was reviving is older than Greece. It goes back to the Indus Valley and to the origin of cloth.
Part of an ongoing series on fashion, history, and whose stories get told.
Collage work by @juliewolfeartist
@metgalaofficial_ @metcostumeinstitute
#MetGala #CostumeArt #MetCostumeArt #metgala2026 #arthistory

posts.caption:
Last week I talked about Mesopotamia where cloth was the economy, where 6,000 women worked the textile workshops of Girsu and where cuneiform may have developed in part to track the production of thread.
That story travelled east.
The Indus Valley Civilisation. Modern-day Pakistan, northwest India, northeast Afghanistan. Larger than ancient Egypt and Mesopotamia combined at its peak. And the oldest confirmed source of cotton in the Old World, evidence going back to 6000 BCE, a full millennium before the Harappan cities even existed.
Fashion history mostly starts in Egypt. Sometimes Mesopotamia. The Indus Valley predates both for cotton, was larger than both at its peak, and produced the draped garment that over a billion people still wear today.
Three reasons it dropped out of the story: the script has never been deciphered. The major excavations were conducted by British colonial administrators in the 1920s. After partition in 1947, the two largest sites ended up in Pakistan while most of the scholarly infrastructure remained in India.
The one I keep finding when I look east instead of west. This is part of an ongoing series on fashion, history, and whose stories get told.

posts.caption:
I thought I‚Äôd imagined this film.
Nobody I knew had seen it, nobody ever mentioned it, but it stayed lodged in my memory for decades! That strange, beautiful, fever dream of an animated movie from my Australian childhood.
When I finally looked into its backstory I couldn‚Äôt believe what I found.
And then I couldn‚Äôt believe the question nobody had thought to ask about it.
Swipe. Then go watch Lindsay Ellis‚Äôs (@namebrandlindsay28) brilliant video essay ‚ÄúDid Disney Really Steal Aladdin?‚ÄĚ for the full production history.
Then come back and let me know your thoughts! ūüí≠

posts.caption:
We surveyed hundreds of Vogue and GQ readers about their wants from #AI in fashion. The key takeaway? Consumers are cautious, and the execution of early applications of AI in fashion is what‚Äôs holding them back right now. Those who have adopted its use for shopping are driven primarily by convenience and efficiency, but many are constrained by concerns around trust and authenticity. posts.caption:
What happens when you put Meryl Streep and Anna Wintour‚ÄĒthe two Mirandas, as it were‚ÄĒin a room together? A wide-ranging conversation about fashion, family, friendship, and yes, the forthcoming sequel to ‚ÄúThe Devil Wears Prada.‚ÄĚ
‚ÄúIn terms of Miranda, and coming back to that character 20 years later, I did think honestly about Anna, and tried to imagine what it was like to carry her responsibility and to be as interested in the world and curious as she must have to be,‚ÄĚ Streep tells Greta Gerwig in Vogue‚Äôs May 2026 cover story. And what did Wintour do when she heard the rumours that ‚ÄúThe Devil Wears Prada 2‚ÄĚ was happening? She called Streep, of course. ‚ÄúI knew she would tell me if it was going to be all right,‚ÄĚ Wintour says. ‚ÄúI trusted her implicitly.‚ÄĚ
Read the full story at the link in bio.

posts.caption: The city of Huron, California, has named May 3rd Willy Chavarria (@newyorkwilly) Day to honor his contributions to fashion, art, and culture more broadly. posts.caption:
Never one to make a timid entrance, @Madonna made a big statement on the 2026 #MetGala red carpet. There was the witchy dress by @ysl, the tumble of raven hair, and the seven ladies-in-waiting carrying her diaphonous lavender cape, a reinterpretation of the 1945 oil painting ‚ÄúThe Temptation of St. Anthony,‚ÄĚ by one of the singer‚Äôs favorite artists, the British-Mexican Surrealist Leonora Carrington. All of this seemingly in service of the dramatic centerpiece: a towering headpiece featuring a skeletal ship dripping in tulle and beads. The hat once belonged to the infamous fashion editor Isabella Blow, and was commissioned by her from fashion‚Äôs go-to milliner, Philip Treacy, who says it‚Äôs among his favorite creations.
Tap the link in our bio for all the details, including more on  the history the chapeau has with Vogue itself.

@maudeapatow is ready now. Fresh off her return in the highly anticipated third season of #Euphoria, Apatow is stepping into a new chapter with the kind of clarity and control that only comes from growing up in real time‚ÄĒon-screen, off-screen, and alongside co-stars who have gone on to navigate intergalactic levels of fame and careers of their own. "Just thinking about us being little kids and having no idea what was in store to now‚Ä¶ It's insane," she says.
In our May cover story, Apatow opens up about the roles that shaped her, the relationships that fulfilled her, the theater work that grounded her, and the rituals‚ÄĒtravel, matcha, and all‚ÄĒthat keep her moving forward. Read our full interview with the actress and director at the link in our bio.

The annual expo in Geneva, Switzerland, brings together the watch industry‚Äôs biggest players to unveil their most wondrous, whimsical, and world-class creations.
